The site originally consisted of concentric rings of timber posts and sarsen stones, with its final phase featuring 42 sarsen stones forming a boundary ring 40 meters in diameter. The Sanctuary is connected to the Avebury henge via the West Kennet Avenue, a 2.4-kilometer-long alignment of standing stones, suggesting a ritual or ceremonial link between the two sites.

Constructed in multiple phases beginning around 3000 BCE, the Sanctuary's purpose remains uncertain, though it may have served as a mortuary house, a high-status dwelling, or a ritual site. Excavations in 1930 revealed postholes, Neolithic pottery, animal bones, and the burial of a young girl, possibly a sacrificial offering. The site was largely destroyed by farmers in the 18th century, but antiquarian William Stukeley documented its layout and speculated on its symbolic significance. Today, the site is marked by concrete posts, and while visually modest, it holds significant archaeological and cultural importance as part of the Avebury complex.

The Future Strategic Tanker Aircraft (FSTA) is a Private Finance Initiative (PFI) program to replace the Royal Air Force's aging Vickers VC10 and Lockheed TriStar tanker fleet. The £10.5 billion contract, awarded in 2008, involves 14 Airbus A330-200 aircraft (designated Voyager) converted for air-to-air refueling and transport. Operated by the AirTanker consortium, the fleet entered service in 2013, with some aircraft available for commercial leasing when not in military use. The program faced delays but became the first UK aircraft project since World War II delivered on time and budget.
Lagoa do Parado Municipal Nature Park is a protected area in Guaratuba, Paraná, characterized by its tropical super-humid climate, rich biodiversity, and significant marine life breeding grounds.

Ali M. Ansari (born 24 November 1967) is a British-Iranian historian specializing in Iranian studies. A professor at the University of St Andrews, he is an expert on modern Iran and has contributed to media outlets including *The Guardian* and *The Independent*. He was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society of Edinburgh in 2016.

Andrea Debono (7 November 1821 – 29 October 1871) was a Maltese trader and explorer in Africa. Active in Sudan and Egypt, he became a British consular agent and explored the White Nile, including the Sobat River. Accused of slave trading, he later retired to Cairo, where he died.
Wilhelm von Henke (1834–1896) was a German anatomist known for his work in anatomy and his theories on the Venus de Milo statue.

Tomás Francisco Reilly (December 20, 1908 – July 21, 1992) was an American-born Catholic bishop. He was the first Bishop of San Juan de la Maguana in the Dominican Republic, serving from 1969 to 1977. Earlier, he was Titular Bishop of Themisonium and Prelate of San Juan de la Maguana from 1956.

The 2018 Atlanta United FC season marked a significant year for the Major League Soccer (MLS) club, culminating in their first MLS Cup championship. Under the leadership of owner Arthur Blank, president Darren Eales, and head coach Gerardo Martino, the team finished the regular season with a strong performance, securing 2nd place in both the Eastern Conference and overall standings. The season was highlighted by standout player Josef Martínez, who emerged as the top goalscorer with 31 goals in league play and 35 overall. Atlanta United also achieved impressive attendance figures, with a record high of 73,019 fans at a home match against the Portland Timbers. The team faced challenges in the U.S. Open Cup, reaching the Round of 16, but ultimately celebrated their success in the MLS Cup Playoffs, where they claimed the championship title. The season was characterized by strategic roster management, including the use of international roster slots and player loans to optimize team performance.

Marcus Furius Camillus (fl. 1st century AD) was a Roman consul in 8 AD and later proconsul of Africa (17–18 AD), where he suppressed the revolt of Tacfarinas. A member of the once-prominent Furii Camilli family, he was the first in centuries to restore its military reputation. After his victory in Africa, Emperor Tiberius granted him triumphal honors. His daughter, Livia Medullina Camilla, was betrothed to Claudius but died before their wedding, and his son, Lucius Arruntius Camillus Scribonianus, later led a failed rebellion against Claudius.

Budha Subba Temple, located in Dharan, Sunsari District, Nepal, is a significant Hindu shrine known for its rich mythology and historical importance. The temple is named after a legendary hunter, Budha Subba, who, after a fateful hunting incident, buried his slingshot and began meditating at the site where the temple now stands. This location is also believed to be the tomb of Budhhi Karna Khebang, the last Limbu King, who was assassinated in 1773, and whose spirit is revered by local Limbu people. Additionally, the temple is associated with various myths, including those of Lord Shiva and Ekalavya, emphasizing its spiritual significance. Visitors traditionally wrote their names on bamboo trees near the temple as a symbol of love, although this practice has been discontinued to protect the bamboo's growth. Today, worshippers tie sacred threads to the bamboo after offering prayers, believing that their wishes will be fulfilled.

The LMS Hughes Crab was a class of 2-6-0 steam locomotives designed by George Hughes for the London, Midland and Scottish Railway (LMS). Introduced in the 1920s, the design featured advanced components for its time, including long travel valves and a new boiler. Despite modifications by Henry Fowler, the locomotives performed well, particularly in Scotland, where they were favored for heavy mineral work. Nicknamed "Crabs" due to their appearance or motion, the class was later succeeded by the LMS Stanier Mogul. Withdrawn by 1967, three examples have been preserved.

Jefferson Joseph DeBlanc (February 15, 1921 – November 22, 2007) was a United States Marine Corps colonel and a Medal of Honor recipient for his actions during World War II. A fighter pilot in the Pacific Theater, he earned the Medal of Honor for shooting down five Japanese aircraft in a single mission over the Solomon Islands on January 31, 1943, despite being wounded and forced to bail out. After evading capture with the help of indigenous islanders, he was rescued and returned to combat, finishing the war with nine aerial victories.  

After the war, DeBlanc earned multiple degrees in education and taught math and physics. He remained in the Marine Corps Reserve until 1972, retiring as a colonel. He later appeared on the History Channel series *Dogfights*, recounting his wartime experiences. DeBlanc was the last surviving Medal of Honor recipient from Louisiana during World War II. He died in 2007 and was buried in Saint Martinville, Louisiana.
*Odostomia farallonensis* is a species of small sea snail in the family Pyramidellidae. It is found in the Pacific Ocean near California's Farallon Islands.

*The Art of Self-Defense* is a 2019 American dark comedy film written and directed by Riley Stearns. It stars Jesse Eisenberg, Alessandro Nivola, and Imogen Poots. The film premiered at South by Southwest on March 10, 2019, and was released in the United States on July 12, 2019, by Bleecker Street.
Yambio Airport (HSYA) is a public airport serving Yambio, South Sudan.

The Sabre Wildcat is an ultralight aircraft featuring a cable-braced high-wing, weight-shift controls, and a tandem two-seat open cockpit. Designed for affordability, its Elite model was the least expensive two-seat aircraft in 2000.
Sergio Ferrer (born 1951) is a Puerto Rican former Major League Baseball shortstop.

Mafdet was an ancient Egyptian goddess associated with justice, execution, and protection. Often depicted as a feline (such as a cheetah or serval), a mongoose, or a woman with such an animal's head, she was believed to defend Ra from threats during his nightly journey and aid in the resurrection of Osiris. Mafdet was also linked to Anubis in royal tombs, suggesting a role in hunting or executing wrongdoers. She was said to rip out the hearts of the guilty, presenting them to the pharaoh. In the New Kingdom, she was thought to rule over a judgment hall in the afterlife, where enemies of the pharaoh were decapitated with her claw. Her imagery frequently appeared on executioners' staffs, emphasizing her fierce and protective nature.
Marko Čakarević (born May 13, 1988) is a Serbian professional basketball player. He has won gold medals for Serbia's youth national teams.

Alfred Lubbock (31 October 1845 – 17 July 1916) was an English sportsman who played first-class cricket for Kent County Cricket Club and appeared in the 1875 FA Cup Final for Old Etonians. He was considered a talented batsman, with his *Wisden* obituary claiming he could have played Test cricket and rivaled W. G. Grace. Two of his brothers, Nevile and Edgar, also played first-class cricket for Kent.

Léonie d'Aunet (2 July 1820 – 3 March 1879) was a French author, playwright, and travel writer, known for being the first woman to participate in a scientific Arctic expedition. She gained recognition for her travelogue *Voyage d'une femme au Spitzberg* and her novels, plays, and journalistic contributions. D'Aunet's personal life included a notable liaison with Victor Hugo, which inspired his poetry but also led to public scandal.

Theodorick Bland (December 2, 1708 – June 1, 1784) was a Virginia planter, politician, and militia colonel. He represented Isle of Wight, Surry, and Prince George counties in the Virginia Senate from 1776 to 1779. Bland owned the Cawsons and Kippax plantations.

*Pinstripe* is a puzzle-adventure video game developed and published by Atmos Games, led by Thomas Brush, who created its art, music, programming, and writing. Released on April 25, 2017, the game follows ex-minister Teddy as he journeys through a surreal, hellish world to rescue his kidnapped daughter, Bo, from the enigmatic Pinstripe. Funded through a successful Kickstarter campaign, *Pinstripe* features voice acting, a unique art style, and an original score. The game received mixed reviews, with praise for its atmosphere and themes but criticism of its narrative and gameplay mechanics.

FC Stal Kamianske was a Ukrainian football club founded in 1926, originally as FC Metalist Kamianske. The club underwent several name changes, including FC Dzerzhynka and Stal Dniprodzerzhynsk, and achieved notable success in the Soviet era, finishing as a runner-up in the Ukrainian Championship in 1938. After various reorganizations, it was reestablished in 1998 as FC Stal Dniprodzerzhynsk and later promoted to the Ukrainian Premier League in 2015. The club was renamed FC Stal Kamianske in 2016 but dissolved in 2018 due to financial difficulties.

Erik Rauch (May 15, 1974 – July 13, 2005) was an American biophysicist and theoretical ecologist known for founding MetaCarta and contributing to spatial information processing systems. He earned degrees from Yale University, Stanford University, and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, and held positions at several prestigious institutions before his untimely death in a hiking accident.

Duško Knežević (born May 30, 1959) is a Montenegrin economist, businessman, and academic. He is the founder and president of the Atlas Group, a conglomerate of 30 companies operating in banking, finance, real estate, tourism, education, and media. Knežević also founded Mediterranean University, the first private university in Montenegro, where he serves as president of the board, and the Belgrade Banking Academy. He holds degrees in finance, banking, and law from the University of Belgrade and lectures on finance at UNION University.

Knežević has been involved in high-profile controversies, including allegations of funding Montenegro's ruling Democratic Party of Socialists (DPS) through illicit cash donations over 25 years. In 2019, he released videos and documents implicating senior officials, including President Milo Đukanović, in corruption and money laundering. These revelations sparked widespread protests in Montenegro, calling for government accountability. Knežević, now based in London, faces charges of money laundering but denies wrongdoing. In response to the political climate, he launched the "Do Slobode" movement in 2019, advocating for the rule of law and anti-corruption reforms, and announced plans to establish a political party.

The Queen's Fort Military Museum is a military museum in Bloemfontein, South Africa, originally built in 1848–1849 as a British fort named after Queen Victoria. It later served as a prison, mental institution, and military headquarters. Declared a heritage site in 1990, the museum now exhibits military history from 1820, including apartheid-era conflicts, and features tanks and artillery on its grounds.

Deskaheh, born Levi General on March 15, 1873, was a Cayuga statesman and hereditary chief who served from 1917 to 1925. He is best known for his advocacy for the rights of the Six Nations, including his efforts to petition the League of Nations and challenge Canadian government policies. Deskaheh's activism included traveling to Europe to garner international support, but he faced significant opposition and was ultimately unable to achieve his goals. He died on June 27, 1925, and is remembered as a significant figure in the fight for Indigenous rights.

Melanie Susan Oxley is an Australian musician, singer-songwriter, and primary school teacher, known for her work in rock, dance pop, and soul pop genres. She began her music career in the 1980s, performing with bands such as Paris Green and The Sparklers, before forming a long-standing duo with Chris Abrahams in 1989. Together, they released several critically acclaimed albums, including *Welcome to Violet* (1992), which was nominated for Best Independent Release at the ARIA Music Awards. Outside of music, Oxley is a primary school teacher and mother of three, residing in Sydney, Australia.

Joseph Heller (born April 10, 1941) is an Israeli zoologist and malacologist specializing in the taxonomy, biogeography, and reproductive biology of gastropods. His research focuses on the natural history of terrestrial and aquatic mollusks, including their adaptations to heat and desiccation, and he has developed biogeographic models for studying faunal responses to climate change. Since 1979, he has served as the academic curator of the National Molluscs Collection at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem. Heller has held leadership roles in scientific organizations, including as head of the Department of Zoology at Hebrew University and editor-in-chief of *The Israel Journal of Zoology*. In 2010, he was named an Honorary Member of the Zoological Society of Israel for his contributions to zoological research and education.

The 1952 Mysore State Legislative Assembly election was held on 26 March 1952 to elect all 99 members to the Mysore Legislative Assembly. The voter turnout was 50.38%. K. Chengalaraya Reddy was Chief Minister before the election and K. Hanumanthaiah of INC was elected Chief Minister.

Tim Peters is a German political scientist and politician. He studied law and political science at multiple universities, earning a PhD in political science with a dissertation on the anti-fascist tradition of the Party of Democratic Socialism.

Peters has worked as a legal advisor and official for various organizations, including the CDU/CSU faction in the German Bundestag, General Electric, the Federal Ministry of Finance, and the European Parliament.

He was politically active from a young age, joining the Junge Union and later becoming its chairman in Berlin. In 2000, he was elected "Internet Chancellor" on the platform dol2day. He ran for the CDU in the 2005 German federal election and founded the Brussels branch of the Junge Union, becoming its chairman in 2013.
Reliance was an American automobile manufacturer founded in 1904 in Detroit, Michigan, producing cars and trucks before its 1909 acquisition by General Motors.

Lloyd Quinan (born 1957) is a Scottish politician, television producer, presenter, director, and trained actor. He was a Member of the Scottish Parliament (MSP) for the West of Scotland region from 1999 to 2003, representing the Scottish National Party (SNP). During his time as an MSP, he served as the SNP's Deputy spokesperson for Social Inclusion and was involved in several cross-party groups.

Andy González (January 1, 1951 – April 9, 2020) was an American bassist and a key figure in Afro-Cuban jazz and salsa. Born in New York City, he co-founded influential bands such as Conjunto Libre, Grupo Folklórico y Experimental Nuevayorquino, and The Fort Apache Band with his brother, Jerry González. A sought-after session musician, he performed with legends like Tito Puente, Eddie Palmieri, and Dizzy Gillespie. Known for his mastery of the *tumbao* rhythm, González blended jazz with Cuban and Puerto Rican styles. In 2016, he released his debut solo album, *Entre Colegas*, which earned a Grammy nomination for Best Latin Jazz Album. He died in the Bronx at age 69 from complications of diabetes and pneumonia.

The General der Nachrichtenaufklärung Training Referat was a German military signals intelligence training unit during World War II. It provided specialized instruction in cryptanalysis, radio monitoring, and cipher techniques to personnel in the Wehrmacht's signal intelligence divisions. Courses included cryptanalysis of substitution and transposition ciphers, machine ciphers, and language-specific training for intercept operators. The program aimed to address shortages in skilled cryptanalysts and improve field intelligence operations, though effectiveness declined later in the war due to organizational disruptions.

The laminar flamelet model is a combustion model used to describe turbulent flames as an ensemble of thin, laminar, one-dimensional flamelet structures. It simplifies the complex interactions in turbulent non-premixed combustion by assuming that the local flame front behaves like a steady, laminar flame. The model relies on parameters such as mixture fraction and scalar dissipation to calculate species mass fractions and temperature fields. While it effectively captures the coupling between chemical reactions and molecular transport, it has limitations, such as neglecting transient and curvature effects. Variants like the transient laminar flamelet model address these shortcomings.

Kingman is a city in Mohave County, Arizona, United States. Incorporated in 1952, it serves as the county seat. As of 2020, the population was 32,689. Kingman is located along Interstate 40 and U.S. Route 93 and its motto is "The Heart of Historic Route 66".

Lincoln Home National Historic Site, located in Springfield, Illinois, preserves the only home owned by Abraham Lincoln, where he lived from 1844 until his presidency. Established on August 18, 1971, the site includes the restored Lincoln Home and several adjacent structures, showcasing Lincoln's life and legacy.

"Bizarre Love Triangle" is a single by the English rock band New Order, released on November 5, 1986, as part of their album *Brotherhood*. The song is characterized by its new wave and synth-pop sound, featuring a blend of sequenced instruments and prominent synthesizer elements. The 12-inch version was remixed by Shep Pettibone and has appeared on various compilations, including *Substance* and *Best Of*. The music video, directed by Robert Longo, showcases surreal imagery inspired by his "Men in the Cities" series, including scenes of individuals in business attire flying through the air.

Critically acclaimed since its release, "Bizarre Love Triangle" has been described as a "synth-pop masterpiece" by *Billboard* and recognized as one of New Order's finest works. It has received high rankings in various music lists, including being placed at No. 204 in Rolling Stone's "The 500 Greatest Songs of All Time."

L. David Mech (born January 18, 1937) is an American biologist and ecologist renowned for his research on wolf behavior and ecology. He earned his B.S. from Cornell University (1958) and Ph.D. from Purdue University (1962), where he studied wolves on Isle Royale, leading to his first book, *The Wolves of Isle Royale* (1966). Mech later conducted long-term research in Minnesota’s Superior National Forest and on Ellesmere Island, where he observed wolves with minimal fear of humans, providing unique insights into pack dynamics.  

A senior research scientist with the U.S. Geological Survey and adjunct professor at the University of Minnesota, Mech has authored or co-authored over 380 scientific papers and 11 books, including *The Wolf: The Ecology and Behavior of an Endangered Species* (1970) and *Wolves: Behavior, Ecology, and Conservation* (2003). His work has influenced wolf conservation and management policies. An advocate for regulated hunting and trapping, Mech supports state-managed wolf populations where they are no longer endangered. He remains an active researcher and writer on wolf ecology.

"Stay" is a 1990 single by American singer Glenn Jones from his album *All for You*. Written by Mark Stevens and Bobby Khozouri, the song features a blend of R&B and pop elements. It was later covered by British girl group Eternal in 1993, achieving significant chart success.

Hiroko Anzai (born February 9, 1979, in Kanagawa, Japan) is a Japanese actress, model, and former gravure idol. She debuted in 1996 and gained popularity in the late 1990s as a fashion model and television personality. Known for her work in teen magazines and music collaborations, Anzai briefly retired from show business before returning in 2008. She is also skilled in judo and swimming.

KGGF (690 AM) is a talk radio station licensed to Coffeyville, Kansas, serving Southeast Kansas, Eastern Oklahoma, Southwest Missouri, and Northwest Arkansas. Established in 1930, it originally broadcast at 1010 kHz before moving to its current frequency of 690 kHz in 1941 under the North American Regional Broadcasting Agreement. The station operates at 10,000 watts during the day and 5,000 watts at night. Owned by SEK Media, LLC, KGGF features a mix of local news, farm reports, and nationally syndicated conservative talk shows, with hosts such as Sean Hannity and Dave Ramsey. Weekend programming includes specialty shows on gardening, technology, and old-time radio.

John James Hughes (1814 – June 17, 1889) was a Welsh engineer and businessman best known for founding the city of Donetsk in Ukraine. Initially starting his career in Wales, Hughes gained prominence through his innovations in armaments and armor plating. In 1869, he moved to Russia, where he established metal works that led to the rapid development of the settlement Hughesovka, which later became Donetsk. Hughes's contributions included building infrastructure such as schools and hospitals, and his legacy continued through his sons after his death.

Stewardson Township is a township in Potter County, Pennsylvania, settled in 1852 and incorporated in 1856. As of the 2020 census, it has a population of 64 residents and covers an area of 74.06 square miles.

Ernie Camacho (born February 1, 1956) is an American former professional baseball pitcher. He played in MLB for the Oakland Athletics (1980), Pittsburgh Pirates (1981), Cleveland Indians (1983–1987), Houston Astros (1988), San Francisco Giants (1989–1990), and St. Louis Cardinals (1990).
Ruth Cameron was a singer, producer, and Charlie Haden's manager.

William Horsell (31 March 1807 – 23 December 1863) was a British hydrotherapist, publisher, and activist for temperance and vegetarianism. He co-founded the Vegetarian Society and published early vegetarian literature.

The Maasai Mara conservancies are privately managed wildlife conservation areas surrounding the Maasai Mara National Reserve in Kenya. These conservancies aim to balance wildlife conservation with sustainable tourism and the needs of the local Maasai communities. Tourism in the conservancies is highly regulated, with strict limits on tourist accommodations and safari vehicles, and activities such as night drives, off-road driving, and walking safaris are permitted, unlike in the national reserve. However, tensions have arisen between Maasai pastoralists and tourism operators due to issues like cattle grazing in restricted areas and allegations of unfair profit distribution and exclusion of local communities from decision-making processes, leading to growing distrust in conservancy management.

Pilar is a 1st class municipality in the province of Sorsogon, Philippines, with a population of 75,793 as of the 2020 census. It is divided into 49 barangays and is known for its proximity to whale shark sightings in Pilar Bay, near San Antonio. The local government, in collaboration with WWF, has implemented guidelines to protect the sharks, though enforcement remains inconsistent.

Maximum PC is a monthly American magazine focused on computing, first published in August 1996 under the name "boot" and rebranded as Maximum PC in September 1998. Based in San Francisco and published by Future US, the magazine features product reviews, ratings, and technology news, with a unique editorial style that includes humorous "Pros and Cons" sections in its reviews. Notably, the magazine has awarded a rare "11" rating to the game Half-Life 2 in January 2005 and recognizes outstanding products with a "Kick Ass" award. As of 2011, Maximum PC had a circulation of approximately 192,611. The magazine also offers a free online archive of back issues dating back to December 2003. In addition to its main publication, Maximum PC launched a quarterly spin-off called Maximum Tech in September 2010, which focused on consumer technology but ceased publication in 2011. An Italian edition was briefly published from December 2004 to 2005.

The center of gravity (CG) of an aircraft is a critical factor in flight stability and control. It must remain within manufacturer-specified limits to ensure safe operation. The CG is calculated along the longitudinal axis (fore-aft balance), and in some helicopters, lateral balance is also considered. An improperly loaded aircraft—either overweight or with a CG outside allowable limits—can lead to severe handling issues, reduced control effectiveness, or even loss of flight control. Fuel consumption and weight distribution must be carefully managed to maintain balance throughout the flight.
The Bottle & Fresh Horses is a 1997 studio album by The Refreshments, blending heartland rock with elements of country and power-pop.
Matthijs van Nieuwkerk (born September 8, 1960) is a Dutch journalist and television presenter. He is known for hosting *De Wereld Draait Door*, *Holland Sport*, *Popquiz*, and *Top 2000 à Go-Go*.

An amnesty bin is a container for the disposal of items without risk of penalty. They are used at airports to prevent the spread of invasive species or to allow travelers to discard cannabis. They are also used at music venues for illegal drugs and in Amazon fulfillment centers for damaged or unscannable items.

The Indian River is a river in Lanark County, Ontario, Canada, that flows from an unnamed lake in Lanark Highlands to its mouth at the Mississippi River in Mississippi Mills. Part of the Saint Lawrence River drainage basin, the river passes through notable landmarks such as the Mill of Kintail Conservation Area, which houses museums dedicated to R. Tait McKenzie and James Naismith.

The 2009 FIA GT3 European Championship saw the introduction of new manufacturers like Audi, with their R8 LMS, and Alpina, with their B6. Ferrari replaced its F430 GT3 with the 430 Scuderia GT3, and Morgan replaced its Aero 8 with the Aero Super Sport.

Changes were made to team structures, limiting teams to two cars each, while manufacturers were still allowed a maximum of six cars. Points were awarded to the top eight finishers in each race, and Manufacturer Cups were open to manufacturers supplying at least two teams for the full season.

Alexis Erica Cohen (October 17, 1983 – July 5, 2009) was an American singer and aspiring veterinarian best known for her appearances on *American Idol* in 2008 and 2009. She gained attention for her outspoken reactions to criticism from the judges. Cohen died in a hit-and-run incident in Seaside Heights, New Jersey, in 2009.

The Kilwa Sultanate (957–1513) was a medieval Swahili Muslim kingdom centered on the island of Kilwa Kisiwani, off the coast of present-day Tanzania. Founded by Persian prince Ali ibn al-Hassan Shirazi, it grew into a major trading empire, controlling gold and ivory routes from Sofala to Zimbabwe. At its peak, Kilwa dominated the Swahili Coast, including Zanzibar, Mombasa, and parts of Madagascar. The sultanate's prosperity relied on Indian Ocean trade, connecting Africa with Arabia, Persia, and India. Internal power struggles weakened Kilwa in the late 15th century, and it fell to Portuguese forces in 1505. Kilwan coins found in Australia suggest far-reaching trade networks. The sultanate's legacy endures in Swahili culture and coastal architecture.
**Wilkinson Heights** is a census-designated place (CDP) in Orangeburg County, South Carolina, United States. The population was 1,946 at the 2020 census.

The House of Zogu is an Albanian dynasty founded in the 13th century. Its most famous member, Zog I, proclaimed himself King of the Albanians in 1928 and ruled until 1939. The current head of the house is Crown Prince Leka II, who has no sons.
Olia Lialina (born May 4, 1971) is a Russian internet artist, net art pioneer, and theorist, known for her influential work *My Boyfriend Came Back from the War*.

Elizabeth Parr-Johnston CM (born 1939) is an American-Canadian economist and educator who served as the 16th President of the University of New Brunswick from 1996 to 2002. She previously held the position of President at Mount Saint Vincent University and has taught at several Canadian universities. Parr-Johnston has also worked in the federal civil service and the private sector, notably as Chief of Staff to the Canadian Minister of Employment and Immigration. In recognition of her contributions to education, she was appointed a Member of the Order of Canada in 2008.

"Sendiri" is a 1984 studio album by Indonesian pop singer Chrisye, marking a departure from his previous art rock style. Produced by Addie MS, it features the titular track and other singles, winning the BASF Award for best-selling album.
Herbert Willoughby Ellis was a British entomologist and naturalist known for his extensive collection.

"Cookie" is a single by the South Korean girl group NewJeans, released on August 1, 2022, as part of their self-titled EP. The song has received critical acclaim for its unique sound but has also faced controversy over perceived sexual innuendos in its lyrics, which some critics found inappropriate given the group's age.

HMS *Fairy* was a Fairfield three-funnel, 30-knot destroyer built for the Royal Navy in 1897. Assigned to the Channel Fleet after commissioning in 1898, she operated mainly in Home Waters. During World War I, she served in the 8th and 7th Destroyer Flotillas. On May 31, 1918, while escorting a convoy, *Fairy* rammed and sank the German submarine UC-75, but sustained heavy damage and sank shortly after.

Władysław Studnicki (1867–1953) was a Polish politician, publicist, and political activist. Initially a socialist, he later embraced nationalism and advocated for Polish-German cooperation. He envisioned a Central European bloc against the Soviet Union. In 1939, he opposed the alliance with Britain, warning of dire consequences for Poland. During World War II, he attempted to influence German policy and was imprisoned. After the war, he lived in exile, defending his controversial views.

In mathematics, the dimension of a vector space is the number of vectors in a basis of the space, which is a set of linearly independent vectors that span the entire space. The dimension depends on the base field over which the vector space is defined. For example, the complex numbers form a vector space of dimension 2 over the real numbers but have dimension 1 over the complex numbers. Finite-dimensional vector spaces with the same dimension are isomorphic, meaning they share the same structure.

The concept of dimension extends to other mathematical structures, such as modules, abelian groups, and commutative rings, with related notions like rank and Krull dimension. Generalizations also include infinite-dimensional spaces, trace class operators, and applications in representation theory, where dimension connects to characters and twisted dimensions.

Declo is a small city in Cassia County, Idaho, United States, with a population of 343 as of the 2010 census. Originally named Marshfield, it was renamed in 1916. The J.R. Simplot Company was founded nearby in 1923.
Idan Roll (born April 27, 1984) is an Israeli politician. He was first elected to the Knesset in 2019 as part of the Blue and White alliance, and later joined Yesh Atid. He is married to Harel Skaat.
Eddy L. Harris (born January 26, 1956) is an American writer and filmmaker known for his travel memoirs exploring African-American identity.
Mary McIvor (1897–1941) was an American actress known for her roles in silent Westerns, notably alongside William S. Hart. She married actor William Desmond in 1919.

Andrea Quynhgiao Nguyen (born 1969) is a Vietnamese-American food writer, cookbook author, and authority on Asian cuisines, particularly Vietnamese cooking. Born in Hải Dương, Vietnam, she fled to the United States with her family in 1975. Nguyen's cookbooks, including *Into the Vietnamese Kitchen* and *The Pho Cookbook*, have received critical acclaim, with the latter earning a James Beard Award in 2018. She has contributed to major publications, taught cooking classes, and writes a monthly column for *Cooking Light*.

The Queen Sirikit Museum of Textiles, located in the Grand Palace, Bangkok, was established in 2003 in a renovated building originally known as the Ratsadakorn-bhibhathana Building, which dates back to 1873. The museum showcases the history and significance of textiles in Thailand, reflecting Queen Sirikit's dedication to preserving traditional Thai craftsmanship.

Sthanu Ravi Varma, also known by his regnal title Kulasekhara, was a ruler of the Chera Perumal kingdom in present-day Kerala, India, who reigned from 844/45 to 870/71 AD. His rule marked a period of significant cultural and political activity, with his authority centered around the capital Makotai (modern-day Kodungallur). While his kingship was largely ceremonial, local chieftains and Nambudiri-Brahmins wielded substantial political and religious power.

Sthanu Ravi is noted for his association with the Chola king Rajakesari Varma in military campaigns and for fostering advancements in astronomy. His court hosted the astronomer Sankara Narayana, who authored the *Laghu Bhaskariya Vyakha*, a commentary on Bhaskara I's works. The era also saw the issuance of the Quilon Syrian Christian copper plates, which recorded land grants to a Christian church in Kollam.

Sthanu Ravi's reign is referenced in literary works and inscriptions, and his contributions to culture, religion, and science underscore his significance in Kerala's medieval history.

Curtis Knight and the Squires was an R&B band notable for featuring Jimi Hendrix during the mid-1960s. The group released several recordings, including "Hornet's Nest," showcasing Hendrix's early compositions before he left the band in 1966.

*Godzilla vs. Kong (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack)* is the film score for the 2021 monster film *Godzilla vs. Kong*, composed by Tom Holkenborg and released on March 31, 2021, by WaterTower Music. Holkenborg, a fan of Godzilla, began developing the score in 2018, aiming to create original themes that respected the legacy of the titular monsters while distinguishing them from previous works. The score features a hyper-modern style, with Godzilla's theme emphasizing power through lower brass and percussion, while Kong's theme takes an organic, human-like approach interwoven with electronic elements.

The soundtrack includes tracks such as "Pensacola, Florida (Godzilla Theme)" and "Skull Island (Kong Theme)," released ahead of the album. Additional music used in the film but not included in the soundtrack features songs by artists like Bobby Vinton, Elvis Presley, and Judas Priest. For the Japanese release, the band Man with a Mission contributed the track "Into the Deep."

Critical reception was mixed, with praise for the new themes but criticism for the lack of Akira Ifukube's iconic Godzilla motif and the overuse of electronic elements.

Miller Thomson is a Canadian law firm founded in 1957, with 12 offices and over 550 lawyers. It provides services in corporate law, real estate, intellectual property, and other practice areas. Through strategic mergers, the firm has expanded nationally and is recognized in legal rankings such as *Chambers Canada*. It is a member of Multilaw, a global legal network, and maintains an affiliation with French firm FIDAL.

The Deperdussin 1910 monoplane was an early French aircraft designed by Louis Béchereau and produced by Aéroplanes Deperdussin. First flown in October 1910, it was a mid-wing monoplane with a narrow fabric-covered fuselage, wing warping for control, and a distinctive arrow-shaped tail. Early models used an Anzani 35 hp engine, while later variants adopted more powerful Gnome rotary engines. The aircraft was commercially successful, with multiple versions developed, including racing and military variants. It played a key role in early aviation, serving in flight schools and military operations, including with the French and Australian armed forces. The design evolved into the improved Type B (1911) and later the Deperdussin 1912 Racing Monoplane.

The Great Sandy Desert is a vast arid region in Australia, spanning approximately 284,993 square kilometers across Western Australia and the Northern Territory. Characterized by large longitudinal dunes and the Wolfe Creek meteorite impact crater, the desert is sparsely populated, primarily by Indigenous Australian communities, including the Martu and Pintupi. The climate features low, seasonal rainfall and extreme summer temperatures, while the ecosystem supports diverse flora and fauna, including spinifex vegetation and various animal species such as dingoes and bilbies.

The SS William G. Mather is a historic lake freighter launched on May 23, 1925, by Great Lakes Engineering Works and operated by the Cleveland-Cliffs Iron Company. Named after the company's president, it served as the flagship until 1951 and played a significant role during World War II. After being decommissioned in 1980, it was donated for preservation and is now a museum ship located at Dock 32 in Cleveland, Ohio, where it highlights maritime history and technology.

The "Zar je važno da l' se peva ili pjeva... World Tour" is a concert tour by Serbian pop icon Lepa Brena, promoting her album of the same name. The tour began on November 11, 2017, and concluded on August 10, 2022, encompassing 95 shows across Europe, North America, and Australia. It was announced in October 2017 and included notable performances, such as sold-out concerts at Belgrade's Štark Arena.

*The Sight* is a 2007 children's fantasy novel by Erin Hunter, the first book in the *Warriors: Power of Three* series. It follows three young ThunderClan cats—Lionpaw, Hollypaw, and Jaypaw—as they uncover a prophecy foretelling their destiny. The novel became a *New York Times* bestseller and received critical acclaim.

The Maas–Hoffman model is a mathematical model used to analyze crop tolerance to soil salinity. It represents the relationship between crop yield and salinity through two linear equations, with key parameters including maximum yield, salinity threshold, and the slope of the descending line. The model's parameters are determined using regression analysis to optimize data fit. An inverted version of the model is applied to growth factors, such as watertable depth, that negatively impact crop production at low values.

Kingman is a city in Mohave County, Arizona, United States, and serves as the county seat. Incorporated in 1952, it is located along historic Route 66 and is known as "The Heart of Historic Route 66." Founded in 1882 as a railroad siding, Kingman played a significant role in transportation and military history, including hosting the Kingman Army Airfield during World War II. The city has a population of 32,689 as of the 2020 census and features a cold desert climate. Kingman is a hub for transportation, education, and tourism, with connections to major highways, railroads, and nearby attractions.

Konanakunte is a neighborhood in southern Bangalore, Karnataka, India. Once a rural area known for farming, it has transformed into a semi-urban residential locality. As of 2001, it had a population of 13,262 and a literacy rate of 77%. The area faces infrastructure challenges despite rapid urbanization.

Paul L. Smith (June 24, 1936 – April 25, 2012) was an American-Israeli actor known for his roles in films such as *Popeye* (1980) and *Dune* (1984). He began his career with a role in *Exodus* and later starred in a series of films alongside Michael Coby. Smith successfully sued for the right to use his name after it was changed for an American release. He also appeared in various television series throughout his career, which spanned from 1960 to 1999.

Hostile vehicle mitigation (HVM) refers to security measures designed to prevent vehicle-based attacks. These measures include physical barriers like bollards and Jersey barriers, as well as adapted landscape features. HVM systems are crash-tested to resist penetration by vehicles of varying sizes and speeds, according to standards set by accredited testing facilities. A protective layer of HVM is ideally placed around a building or area, with a standoff distance to mitigate explosive blast effects. HVM is increasingly used to protect crowded events and high-value targets from vehicle ramming attacks, while ideally maintaining pedestrian access.

ESET NOD32 is an antivirus software developed by the Slovak company ESET, first released in 1987. Originally a DOS-based program, it evolved into a comprehensive security solution compatible with multiple operating systems, including Windows, macOS, and Linux. Known for its heuristic analysis and lightweight design, NOD32 has received numerous industry certifications and awards for malware detection and system protection.
The Roland Space Echo is a series of tape delay effects units produced by the Roland Corporation from 1973 to 1990. The most well known models are the RE-101 and RE-201, released in 1974.

The Störtebeker Festival is an annual open-air theatre festival in Ralswiek on the island of Rügen, Germany. Since 1993, it has told the legend of the pirate Klaus Störtebeker, who sailed the Baltic Sea in the 14th century. The festival employs over 140 extras, 20 actors, and 30 horses. By the end of the 2016 season, it had attracted over 7.3 million visitors.

Alexander Edwin Michael "Mike" Keeping (22 August 1902 – 28 March 1984) was an English footballer and manager. He played as a full-back for Southampton and Fulham, making over 280 appearances for the former. He later managed Real Madrid and Heracles Almelo.

Samuel Lord (1803–1889) was a British-American entrepreneur and co-founder of the iconic American department store Lord & Taylor. Born in Saddleworth, Yorkshire, he emigrated to the United States in 1826, establishing a dry goods store in Manhattan that evolved into the renowned retail chain. After retiring in 1862, he returned to England, where he pursued horticulture until his death in 1889.

The 1944 explosion in Aarhus occurred on July 4, 1944, at the Port of Aarhus in Denmark, when a barge loaded with 150 tonnes of ammunition detonated at 13:47. The explosion generated a massive column of water and was heard up to 20 kilometers away, causing significant destruction in the surrounding area. It resulted in the deaths of 39 individuals, including 33 Danes, primarily dock workers, and injured approximately 250 others. The blast also caused extensive damage to buildings and infrastructure, with debris raining down over a kilometer radius. Following the incident, German authorities attributed the explosion to communist sabotage, a claim contested by Danish labor unions. A memorial service was held at Aarhus Cathedral, and a monument was erected in Vestre Cemetery in December 1945 to honor the victims, 12 of whom were buried in a mass grave. Donations to support the victims' families totaled 522,000 kroner.

The S9 is a line of the Zürich S-Bahn, operated by the Zürcher Verkehrsverbund (ZVV). It runs from Schaffhausen to Uster via Zürich Hauptbahnhof, with trains operating every 30 minutes. Services are operated using Re 450 locomotives with double-deck coaches. Since 2015, its western terminus was shortened from Zug to Uster.

Helene Hewitt OBE is a British climate scientist specializing in ocean and sea ice modeling. She is the Head of Ocean Modelling at the Met Office Hadley Centre, where she develops climate models, including HadGEM3. Hewitt contributed to the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report and was honored with an OBE in 2022.

Daniël Termont (born May 19, 1953) is a Belgian politician who served as the Mayor of Ghent from 2007 to 2018. A member of the Socialist Party (SP.a), Termont began his political career at the age of fourteen, becoming actively involved in the socialist movement and later holding various leadership roles within the party. He was first elected as a city councillor in 1976 and has maintained a continuous presence in local politics since then. Termont served as an alderman for the port, economic development, and utilities before being elected mayor in 2007, succeeding Frank Beke.

In addition to his role as mayor, Termont has held several significant positions in the energy sector, including Vice President of FARYS and president of the Vlaamse Energieholding. He is also a board member of the port of Ghent and has been involved with the football club AA Gent. Termont was recognized internationally in 2014 when he was nominated for the World Mayor award, finishing second. Throughout his career, he has received various decorations for his contributions to public service and local governance.

The State Seal of Japan, adopted in 1874, is a pure gold square seal inscribed with "Seal of Great Japan" in seal script. Created by master craftsman Abei Rekido, it is used for official state purposes, such as certifying Japanese orders. Historically significant, the seal featured prominently during the 2019 imperial transition ceremonies.

Primitive Love is the fifth studio album by American band Miami Sound Machine, released on August 13, 1985, by Epic Records. Produced by Emilio Estefan Jr., it marked the group's breakthrough into mainstream success, becoming their first album to chart on the *Billboard* 200, peaking at No. 23. The album features a dance-pop sound and spawned four hit singles, including "Conga," "Bad Boy," and "Words Get in the Way," which reached the Top 10 of the *Billboard* Hot 100. *Primitive Love* was a commercial success, ranking No. 10 on *Billboard*'s 1986 Year-End chart.

Manuel José de Jesus Silva (born April 9, 1946) is a Portuguese former football player and manager, best known for his success as a coach, particularly with Egyptian club Al Ahly. As a midfielder, he played for several Portuguese clubs, including Benfica, União Tomar, and Farense, before transitioning to management in 1978. 

José achieved notable success with Al Ahly, leading the team to an unprecedented period of dominance, including four CAF Champions League titles, five consecutive Egyptian Premier League titles, and a historic third-place finish at the FIFA Club World Cup in 2006. He also managed clubs such as Sporting CP, Boavista, and Persepolis, and briefly coached the Angola national team. Renowned for his achievements in African football, he was honored with multiple awards, including the CAF Coach of the Year in 2006, and remains a celebrated figure in Egyptian football history.
**Dame Thea King DBE FRCM FGSM (1925–2007) was a British clarinetist and professor at the Royal College of Music.**
The women's hockey teams at the 2010 Commonwealth Games consisted of squads from Australia, Canada, England, India, Malaysia, New Zealand, Scotland, South Africa, Trinidad and Tobago, and Wales.
Hamiota Municipality is a rural municipality in Manitoba, Canada, formed in 2015 through amalgamation.

Wafa Mustafa (born in Masyaf, Syria) is a Syrian journalist and activist. Her activism is largely motivated by the disappearance of her father, Ali Mustafa, a human rights activist who was last seen on July 2, 2013.

Mustafa was politically active from a young age, participating in pro-Palestine demonstrations as a child and later becoming involved in protests during the Syrian Civil War. In September 2011, she was arrested and detained in Damascus. Mustafa, her mother, and her sister fled Syria in July 2013, eventually settling in Berlin. She graduated from Bard College Berlin in 2020. Mustafa continues to advocate for information about her father and other detained refugees, including protesting at the Al Khatib trial in Koblenz, Germany, and briefing the UN Security Council on forced disappearances in Syria.
Mount Gould is a peak in the Sierra Nevada, on the Fresno/Inyo county line.

Milner is a city in Lamar County, Georgia, United States. Founded in 1912 and named after pioneer citizen Willis R. Milner, it spans 2.1 square miles and had a population of 772 as of the 2020 census.

Kate Walsh (née Shimmin, born February 13, 1992) is a former professional netball player from Australia and England, known for her positions as Goal Defender (GD) and Goal Keeper (GK). She began her career with the Adelaide Thunderbirds in 2011, winning a premiership in 2013, and later played for the Queensland Firebirds and Sunshine Coast Lightning. Walsh represented Australia in the Fast5 format from 2012 to 2017 and was a member of the Australian 21/U team. She announced her retirement from elite netball in November 2022.

Juini Booth (February 12, 1948 – July 11, 2021) was an American jazz bassist known for his work on double bass and electric bass. Born in Buffalo, New York, he performed with prominent artists such as Art Blakey, McCoy Tyner, and Sun Ra's Arkestra, where he was a long-serving member. Booth's career spanned collaborations with notable musicians including Freddie Hubbard, Tony Williams, and Elvin Jones, as well as international tours and recordings. He passed away in Buffalo at the age of 73.
Art Quimby (1933–2010) was an American basketball player and educator, known for his record-setting college career at UConn and as a center drafted by the Rochester Royals.

In Eastern Orthodox and Byzantine Catholic liturgical practice, an entrance is a procession of clergy into the sanctuary through the Holy Doors. The Divine Liturgy (Eucharist) features two entrances: the Little Entrance, which occurs during the Liturgy of the Catechumens and symbolizes the Incarnation of Christ, and the Great Entrance, near the beginning of the Liturgy of the Faithful, which symbolizes Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem. Great Vespers also includes an Entrance, similar to the Little Entrance, while the Liturgy of the Presanctified Gifts features a silent Great Entrance. Liturgical scholars view the Entrances as later additions to the Liturgy.

Jawadia Arabic College, established in 1928 by Maulana Syed Mohammad Sajjad Al-Husaini in Banaras, India, focuses on providing religious education in jurisprudence, theology, and literature to Shia Muslim students.

Lead Section:  

*My Mowgli Boy* (Chinese: 我的莫格利男孩) is a 2019 Chinese romantic comedy-drama series starring Ma Tianyu and Yang Zi. The story follows Mo Ge Li (Ma), a forest-raised young man, who is accidentally brought to the city by Ling Xi (Yang), a career-driven woman. As they live together, they develop feelings while learning from each other—Mo Ge Li adapts to modern life, while Ling Xi embraces environmentalism. The series, praised for its eco-conscious themes and fashion aesthetics, aired on iQiyi from August 29 to October 10, 2019, spanning 50 episodes. It features original soundtracks, including Yang Zi's closing theme, *Windy Night*.

William Ross Bond (December 4, 1918 – April 1, 1970) was a United States Army brigadier general who served in World War II, the Korean War, and the Vietnam War. A decorated officer, he was awarded multiple honors, including the Distinguished Service Medal and Silver Star. Bond was killed in action in Vietnam during Operation Toan Thang IV.

Martin Straka (born September 3, 1972) is a Czech former professional ice hockey forward who played in the National Hockey League (NHL) for 15 seasons. Drafted 19th overall by the Pittsburgh Penguins in 1992, he also played for the Ottawa Senators, New York Islanders, Florida Panthers, Los Angeles Kings, and New York Rangers. Straka was known for his speed and two-way play, achieving a career-high 95 points in the 2000–01 season. Internationally, he won gold at the 1998 Winter Olympics and the 2005 World Championships. After returning to the Czech Extraliga in 2008, he won a league title with HC Škoda Plzeň in 2013. Straka retired in 2014.

Augustin Schubert (born František; 1902 – July 28, 1942) was a Czech Augustinian priest known for his opposition to Nazism during the German occupation of Czechoslovakia. Born in Žižkov, Prague, he was fluent in both Czech and German and became a popular preacher. Schubert actively opposed the Nazi regime, delivering a poignant sermon on the day of the occupation. He was arrested by the Gestapo in 1940 and subsequently imprisoned in various concentration camps, where he suffered severe mistreatment. Schubert died in Dachau at the age of 40 and is currently undergoing the process of beatification.

Furcifer tuzetae is a species of chameleon endemic to Madagascar. Known only from a single specimen collected in dry forest near Befandriana Sud, it is oviparous and classified as Data Deficient by the IUCN due to limited data. Threats may include habitat loss from slash-and-burn agriculture and logging. It was first described in 1972 and later reclassified under the genus *Furcifer*.

The karbovanets was a historical currency of Ukraine, used during three distinct periods: the Ukrainian People's Republic (1917–1920), the Nazi occupation (1942–1945), and post-Soviet Ukraine (1992–1996). Initially introduced by the Central Rada in 1917, it was issued in various denominations and designs, including postage stamp currency and state credit notes. During World War II, the Nazi administration issued karbovanets banknotes pegged to the Reichsmark. In independent Ukraine, the karbovanets replaced the Soviet ruble in 1992 but suffered from hyperinflation. It was replaced by the hryvnia in 1996 at a rate of 100,000 karbovantsiv to 1 hryvnia.

The crested servaline genet (Genetta cristata) is a genet species endemic to Nigeria and Cameroon. It is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List because it is threatened by habitat loss. It has khaki colored fur with dark spots, a long tail with black and whitish bands, and a crest on the back of its neck. It inhabits scrub and forests at elevations up to 1,000 m (3,300 ft).
John Birkbeck (dates unknown) was a British Quaker banker, caver, and mountaineer who explored Gaping Gill and co-founded the Alpine Club.

The Slutsk uprising (November 27 – December 31, 1920) was an armed resistance by Belarusian forces against Soviet rule during the aftermath of the Soviet-Polish War and the Russian Civil War. Centered in Slutsk, Belarus, the uprising was led by local militias advocating for Belarusian independence. Despite initial successes, the poorly equipped Belarusian forces were ultimately defeated by the Red Army, forcing them to retreat into Polish territory.
Wat Bakong is a Theravada Buddhist temple in Siem Reap, Cambodia. Originally built in 1939, it has been restored and is considered cultural heritage.

Joshua Oigara (born 1975) is a Kenyan businessman and accountant. He is the former Group Chief Executive Officer of Kenya Commercial Bank Group (KCB), overseeing operations in several East African countries.

Oigara holds a Bachelor of Commerce degree from the University of Nairobi and an MBA from Edith Cowan University. He is also a certified public accountant. Before joining KCB in 2011, he worked at PricewaterhouseCoopers, Bidco Africa, and Bamburi Cement, where he rose to become Group Finance Director. He stepped down as CEO of KCB in May 2022.

Hyoyeon (born Kim Hyo-yeon on September 22, 1989) is a South Korean singer, dancer, DJ, and television personality. She debuted in 2007 as a member of the girl group Girls' Generation under SM Entertainment. Known for her dance skills, she has appeared on shows like *Dancing with the Stars* and *Hit the Stage*. Since 2018, she has pursued a solo career as DJ Hyo, releasing EDM tracks under SM's ScreaM Records. She is also part of subgroups Oh!GG and Got the Beat.

Gavan McCormack is an Australian academic specializing in modern East Asian history, particularly Japan. He has taught at multiple universities, including the Australian National University. McCormack's research interests include political, intellectual, and environmental history. He has also contributed as a guest editorial staff of the South Korean newspaper, Kyunghyang Shinmun.

*The Divine Wings of Tragedy* is the third studio album by American progressive metal band Symphony X, released in November 1996. Blending neoclassical, power, and symphonic metal, the album features musical and lyrical influences from classical composers and literary works. Widely regarded as a landmark in progressive metal, it received critical acclaim and has been included in several "best of" genre lists.

Cephaloridine is a first-generation cephalosporin antibiotic introduced in 1962. It was initially used to treat bacterial infections but declined in human medicine due to nephrotoxicity and poor oral absorption. It remains used in veterinary medicine. The drug is excreted renally and can cause kidney damage by accumulating in proximal tubules. Its structure includes a pyridinium group and a thiophene side chain.

Dacrydium cupressinum, commonly known as rimu, is a slow-growing tree native to New Zealand, reaching heights of up to 50 meters. It thrives in lowland and montane forests and is valued for its timber and ornamental qualities.

Final Account is a 2020 documentary film directed by Luke Holland, featuring interviews with former Nazis and German civilians about their roles in the Holocaust. Produced over a decade, the film explores themes of complicity and memory. It premiered at the Venice Film Festival and received critical acclaim, with a 93% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes. Holland, whose family was affected by the Holocaust, died shortly after completing the film.

Manuel Sager (born 1955) is a Swiss diplomat and lawyer who served as the Swiss Ambassador to the United States (2010–2014) and Director of the Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (2014–2020).
New Berlin is a borough in Union County, Pennsylvania, settled in 1792 and incorporated in 1795, with a population of 801 as of 2020.

WU polyomavirus (WU virus), also known as Human polyomavirus 4, is a species of virus in the family *Polyomaviridae* and genus *Betapolyomavirus*. Discovered in 2007 at Washington University, it was identified through shotgun sequencing of respiratory secretions from a pneumonia patient. The virus has a circular double-stranded DNA genome encoding five viral proteins. WU virus is globally distributed, with seroprevalence rates ranging from 54% to 97% in various populations, suggesting primary infection occurs in childhood. While its pathogenicity remains unclear, it may have potential relevance in immunocompromised individuals.

The Kokerboom–Auas High Voltage Power Line is an 800 km (500 mi), 400kV alternating current (AC) electricity transmission line in Namibia. Construction began in 2021 and is expected to be completed in 2024. It will improve power delivery and reliability, and facilitate power import from South Africa.
Beau Mirchoff (born January 13, 1989) is an American-Canadian actor. He is known for his roles in *Scary Movie 4*, *Desperate Housewives*, *Awkward.*, *The Fosters*, and *Good Trouble*.

Chuck Hunt (March 2, 1954 – December 21, 2021) was the First Gentleman of Massachusetts from April 10, 2001, to January 2, 2003, during the governorship of his wife, Jane Swift. Born in Adams, Massachusetts, he faced health challenges from Alport syndrome throughout his life. Hunt was a stay-at-home father to their three children and played a significant role in supporting his wife's political career. He passed away from kidney disease related to his condition.

Cycling in Prague has evolved from a historically bike-unfriendly city to one with increasing infrastructure and popularity. The first bicycle lanes appeared in 1956, but significant development did not occur until after the Velvet Revolution in 1989, when car traffic surged and leisure cycling gained traction. A plan for 400 km of cycling routes was approved in 1993, though less than half were completed by 2000. As of 2021, four bike-sharing companies operate in the city, and bicycles can be transported for free on various public transport options, including the Metro and some trams.

One Nation Labour was a political concept and rebranding of the British Labour Party under the leadership of Ed Miliband, emphasizing social justice, economic rebalancing, and national unity. Introduced in 2012, it sought to address societal inequalities and promote a fairer, more inclusive Britain. Economically, One Nation Labour advocated for long-term investment, support for low- and middle-income earners, and a focus on technical education to enhance mid-level skills, which Miliband argued were essential for sustainable growth. Socially, it emphasized equalizing life chances, corporate responsibility, and addressing income disparities.

Culturally, One Nation Labour celebrated Britain's diversity while advocating for immigration policies that prioritized the economic interests of British workers, particularly those in lower-income brackets. It also acknowledged the challenges of emigration and migration's impact on communities. The platform supported further devolution of powers to local governments in England, mirroring the arrangements in Scotland and Wales.

Ideologically, One Nation Labour built on and diverged from New Labour's Third Way approach. While endorsing a form of capitalism that worked for the people, it embraced a more public use of the term "socialism," advocating for a fairer and more just society. This marked a shift from New Labour's reluctance to use the term, reflecting a renewed focus on ethical socialism and social democratic values.
*Personal and Vehicular Basic Loads and The Morrow Project Role Playing Expansion* is a role-playing game supplement providing guidelines for character skills, tasks, and item repairs.
Eileen Dennes (1898–1991) was an Irish silent film actress known for her roles in *Sheba* (1919) and *The Pipes of Pan* (1923).

Callington was a former borough constituency in Cornwall, UK, that existed from 1585 until its disfranchisement in 1832. Initially a rotten borough, it was characterized by a small electorate and significant patronage, primarily from the Rolle and Coryton families.

Dongmen (东门镇) is a town in Luocheng Mulao Autonomous County, Guangxi, China. As of 2021, it administers 5 communities and 16 villages. In 2019, its population was 101,483. Tourist attractions include the Martyrs' Cemetery and Kaiyuan Temple.

Dimitar Dobrev (February 12, 1868 – April 11, 1944) was a Bulgarian naval officer who served in the Bulgarian and Russian navies from 1885 to 1914. He participated in several significant conflicts, including the Serbo-Bulgarian War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Balkan Wars. Dobrev held various positions, including commander of the cruiser Nadezhda and head of mobile defense during the First Balkan War. He was recognized for his bravery with multiple awards, including the Bulgarian Order of Saint Alexander and the Russian Order of St. Vladimir. After leaving the navy, he worked as a lawyer in Veliko Tarnovo. A ship in the Bulgarian Navy is named in his honor.

"Something Precious" is a song recorded by American country artist Skeeter Davis. Written by Lorene Mann, it was released in January 1962 as the B-side to "Where I Ought to Be." Unlike typical B-sides, it charted separately, reaching number 23 on the Billboard Hot Country Songs chart. The track later appeared on Davis's 1963 album *Skeeter Davis Sings The End of the World*.

The Wrocław Global Forum (WGF) is an annual international conference held in Wrocław, Poland, since 2010. Organized with support from the Atlantic Council and other institutions, it focuses on transatlantic dialogue, global challenges, and security issues. The event features high-profile speakers, the Atlantic Council's Freedom Awards, and is supported by governments and corporate sponsors.

Eucalyptus largiflorens, commonly known as black box, is a tree species in the family Myrtaceae native to Australia. It grows up to 20 meters tall, with rough, dark grey bark and lance-shaped leaves. Flowering occurs in most months, especially in autumn and spring, producing white flowers. First described in 1855 by Ferdinand von Mueller, the species is widespread across Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia, particularly in grassy woodlands on the floodplains of the Murray–Darling basin. Indigenous Australians utilized its seeds as a food source, processing them to remove bitterness before consumption.

The Apostles' Fast is a period of fasting and prayer observed in various Christian traditions, particularly in the Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, and Eastern Catholic Churches. It commemorates the preparation of the Apostles for their missionary work following the Ascension of Jesus Christ and the Feast of Pentecost. Rooted in Sacred Tradition and scriptural references, the fast is seen as a time of thanksgiving for the Apostles' endurance and dedication.

The fast begins on the Monday after the Sunday of All Saints and ends on June 29, the Feast of Saints Peter and Paul. Its duration varies depending on the date of Pascha (Easter). The fasting rules are less strict than those of Great Lent, with variations across traditions. Oriental Churches, such as the Coptic, Syriac, and Maronite Churches, observe modified versions of the fast, with differing durations and practices.

Todd Compton is an independent researcher and author specializing in Mormon history. He is known for his book, *In Sacred Loneliness: The Plural Wives of Joseph Smith*, which received awards from the John Whitmer Historical Association and the Mormon History Association.

En Avant Guingamp, commonly referred to as Guingamp or EAG, is a French professional football club based in Guingamp, Brittany. Founded in 1912, the club plays its home matches at the Stade de Roudourou, which has a capacity of 19,033, significantly larger than the commune's population. Currently competing in Ligue 2, Guingamp has a storied history, including three promotions to Ligue 1, with their longest top-flight stint lasting from 2013 to 2019. The club's major achievements include winning the Coupe de France twice, in 2009 and 2014, and the 1996 UEFA Intertoto Cup. Known for developing notable players like Didier Drogba and Florent Malouda, Guingamp also fields a women's team in Division 1 Féminine.
The *Dartmouth Jack-O-Lantern* is Dartmouth College's humor magazine, founded in 1908. It publishes quarterly and maintains an active online presence.

The Homilies of Gregory is a Byzantine manuscript commissioned by Patriarch Photios I as a gift for Emperor Basil I, celebrating the triumph of Orthodoxy over Iconoclasm. Renowned for its elaborate design and preservation, the manuscript features homilies by St. Gregory of Nazianzus, accompanied by illustrations that draw parallels between historical heresies and contemporary theological tensions, reflecting Photios's opposition to Latin interpretations of divinity.

Michael Klein (10 October 1959 – 2 February 1993) was a Romanian footballer who played as a left back. He spent most of his club career at Corvinul Hunedoara and Dinamo București, winning a league title and cup with the latter, before moving to Bayer Uerdingen in Germany. Internationally, Klein earned 89 caps for Romania, representing his country at UEFA Euro 1984 and the 1990 FIFA World Cup. Known for his consistency and dedication, he tragically died of a heart attack during training in 1993 at the age of 33. The Stadionul Michael Klein in Hunedoara is named in his honor.

Fairhope Plantation is a historic estate located near Uniontown, Alabama, built between 1857 and 1861 for Joseph Selden and his wife, Elizabeth. Designed by architect Theophilus Gilliam Fowler, the plantation features distinctive Gothic Revival architectural elements, including intricate bargeboards and a cast-iron veranda. Fairhope Plantation was added to the National Register of Historic Places on May 29, 1992, and has remained in the Selden family for over a century, functioning as a farm after the original owners' deaths.

The Queen Sirikit Museum of Textiles is a history and textile museum located within the Grand Palace in Bangkok, Thailand. Originally established in 1873 as the Ratsadakorn-bhibhathana Building, it was intended to centralize Siam's taxing system. In 2003, Queen Sirikit requested permission to renovate the vacant building into a textile museum.

Aleksei Losev (23 September 1893 – 24 May 1988) was a Soviet and Russian philosopher, philologist, and culturologist, known for his works on classical aesthetics, mythology, and the philosophy of language. A critic of Soviet ideology, he was imprisoned in the 1930s but later rehabilitated, continuing his scholarly work under state censorship. His major contributions include *The Dialectics of Myth* and the eight-volume *History of Classical Aesthetics*, for which he received the USSR State Prize in 1986.

Eduard Deisenhofer (27 June 1909 – missing in action 31 January 1945) was a German SS-Obersturmbannführer who served in the Waffen-SS during World War II. He began his career in the SS in 1930 and held various command positions, including service in the SS Division Totenkopf and the SS Division Wiking. Deisenhofer was awarded the Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross for his bravery in combat. He disappeared near Arnswalde, Germany, in January 1945 and was classified as missing in action.
**Saint-Denis–Porte de Paris** is a Paris Métro station on line 13, located in the commune of Saint-Denis, near the Stade de France.

Major General William J. Maddox Jr. (May 22, 1921 – January 5, 2001), nicknamed the "Grey Ghost," was a highly decorated United States Army aviator who served from 1943 to 1976. He participated in World War II, the Korean War, and the Vietnam War, earning numerous awards, including the Army Distinguished Service Medal and the Silver Star. After retiring, Maddox advised King Hussein of Jordan and held leadership roles in aviation companies in the Middle East and Asia. He passed away in Bedford, Texas, and is buried at Arlington National Cemetery.

Old Street is a historic thoroughfare in London, England, with origins dating back to at least the 13th century, and possibly earlier as part of a Roman or pre-Roman route connecting Silchester and Colchester. The street has undergone significant changes, including widening in the 19th century, but retains some 18th- and early 19th-century buildings. Located on the London Inner Ring Road, it forms part of the boundary of the congestion charge zone and is known for its vibrant nightlife, tech industry presence around "Silicon Roundabout," and notable graffiti by artists like Banksy. Old Street station, situated under the roundabout, serves as a key transport hub.
Margit L. McCorkle is an American musicologist known for her catalogues of Brahms and Schumann.

The Integrated Traffic and Landscape Concept (IVL) is a Luxembourgish urban and transport planning strategy developed through interministerial collaboration and expert workshops. It aims to manage urban growth by concentrating development around key municipalities near major transport routes, reducing sprawl and promoting public transit use. The IVL outlines two future scenarios—cross-border commuters and resident workers—and proposes five spatial models, including centralized urban growth, development along transport axes, and regional hubs.

Whiteboard animation is a style of video presentation where illustrations are drawn sequentially on a whiteboard (or a similar surface) and recorded to create an animated effect. Also known as *video scribing* or *animated doodling*, it is commonly used by businesses and educators for marketing, training, and online learning. The process involves scripting, storyboarding, and recording drawings, often sped up in post-production. While popularized by commercials and platforms like YouTube, studies suggest it may not be more effective for learning than other methods. Notable examples include UPS commercials and the *RSA Animate* series, which gained widespread online attention. Software has expanded its variations and accessibility.

Sainte-Geneviève-de-Batiscan is a parish municipality located in the Mauricie region of Quebec, Canada. Established on July 1, 1855, it is situated on the northeast shore of the Batiscan River, near the confluence with the Veillet River. The municipality has a total area of 100.60 km² and a population of 1,028 as of the 2021 Census. Originally named in honor of Saint Genevieve, the area has historical ties to the Algonquin chief Batiscan. Sainte-Geneviève-de-Batiscan served as the seat of Champlain County until 1982 and is now part of the Les Chenaux regional county municipality.

Sid Hudson (January 3, 1915 – October 10, 2008) was a professional baseball pitcher who played in Major League Baseball (MLB) from 1940 to 1954, primarily for the Washington Senators and the Boston Red Sox. A two-time All-Star, Hudson achieved a career record of 104–152 with a 4.28 earned run average and 734 strikeouts. After retiring, he served as a pitching coach and scout, contributing to baseball for several decades.

UPC Nederland was a Dutch telecommunications company founded in 1995 as a joint venture. It acquired many regional TV cable operators before being acquired by Liberty Global in 2014. UPC was superseded by Ziggo in April 2015. It provided cable television, broadband internet, and telephone services.
The Willard Gallery was an art gallery in New York City founded in 1940 by Marian Guthrie Willard. It closed in 1987.

Aadmi is a 1993 Indian Hindi film directed by Arshad Khan, featuring Mithun Chakraborty as Vijay M. Srivastav, an honest man wrongfully imprisoned for the murder of his family. The film follows his struggle against corruption and betrayal.

A composite film is a cinematic work composed of interconnected but self-contained stories, unified by recurring elements such as themes, characters, or settings. Examples include *Pulp Fiction*, *Love Actually*, and *Paris, je t'aime*. These films may use titled segments, varied sequencing, and distinct or unified musical scores to enhance cohesion. The structure draws from literary traditions like composite novels and short story cycles, emphasizing both individual narratives and their collective impact.

DWOW, branded as K-Lite 103.5, is a radio station based in Mandaluyong, Philippines, broadcasting on 103.5 MHz. Owned by Advanced Media Broadcasting System, it originally launched on December 18, 1979, under the call sign DWIM. Over the years, the station has undergone several rebrandings and format changes, including its previous identities as Wow FM and Max FM. Since July 22, 2013, it has operated under the K-Lite branding, focusing on a Hot Adult Contemporary format.
Leucocyanidin is a flavan-3,4-diol compound found in various plants, involved in the biosynthesis of proanthocyanidins and anthocyanins.
R (Corner House Research) v Director of the Serious Fraud Office is a significant legal case concerning the rule of law and the halting of a bribery investigation into BAE Systems.
Ronald Horvath is an American-born geographer and academic. He taught at the University of Sydney from 1976 to 1996 before retiring.
The Light Helicopter Experimental (LHX) program was initiated by the U.S. Army in 1982 to develop a replacement for several existing helicopters, ultimately leading to the RAH-66 Comanche.

*Daniel Isn't Real* is a 2019 American psychological horror film directed by Adam Egypt Mortimer, based on the novel *In This Way I Was Saved* by Brian DeLeeuw. It stars Miles Robbins, Patrick Schwarzenegger, Sasha Lane, and Mary Stuart Masterson. The film follows a troubled college student, Luke (Robbins), who revives his childhood imaginary friend Daniel (Schwarzenegger), only to find that Daniel is a dangerous supernatural entity. *Daniel Isn't Real* premiered at South by Southwest on March 9, 2019, and was released in the United States on December 6, 2019, by Samuel Goldwyn Films.

Powers Auditorium, formerly known as the Warner Theatre, is a Youngstown, Ohio, performing arts venue. Designed by Rapp and Rapp, it opened on May 14, 1931, as part of the Warner Brothers film company's theater chain, built as a memorial to Sam Warner.

The theater operated until 1968, when it was slated for demolition. Following public outcry and a donation from the Edward W. Powers family, it was renovated in 1969 and renamed Powers Auditorium. The auditorium underwent acoustical renovation to become a concert hall, and in September 1969, the Youngstown Symphony performed there. Powers Auditorium was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1980.

Health Canada is the department of the Government of Canada responsible for national health policy, public health, and the regulation of health products and food. Headed by the Minister of Health, it oversees federal health programs, enforces health-related laws, and ensures the safety of consumer products, pharmaceuticals, and medical devices. With an annual budget of $3.9 billion (2021–22) and over 11,000 employees, Health Canada collaborates with partner agencies such as the Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC) and the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR).  

The department enforces compliance with health regulations through its Compliance and Enforcement Directorate, which inspects products like drugs, tobacco, and natural health products. It also administers a Special Access Program for unapproved medications in urgent cases. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Health Canada played a key role in vaccine approvals, including decisions on the AstraZeneca vaccine.  

Health Canada has faced criticism for delays in drug approvals, with only 33% of submissions (2015–2019) meeting its 300-day target. It has also been scrutinized for lax regulation of natural health products and transparency in overseas drug inspections.
