She spent most of her domestic career with the Adelaide Thunderbirds, winning the 2013 premiership, and also played for the Queensland Firebirds and Sunshine Coast Lightning. Walsh represented Australia in Fast5 netball (2012–2017) and later switched to play for England in 2019. She retired from elite netball in 2022 after a career spanning over a decade.

The Wenceslas Bible is a richly illuminated German translation of the Bible, created in the 1390s in Prague, Bohemia, under the patronage of King Wenceslaus IV and his wife, Sophia. Commissioned by Martin Rotlev, a wealthy Prague burgher, it represents one of the earliest German Bible translations from the Latin Vulgate. Despite Emperor Charles IV's earlier ban on vernacular Bible translations, Wenceslaus supported this monumental project, reflecting ties to the Bohemian Reformation. The incomplete manuscript contains 654 miniatures and was intended to be one of the most elaborate medieval manuscripts.

HMS *Modeste* was a 64-gun third-rate ship of the line, launched in 1759 for the French Navy. The Royal Navy captured her later that year. They used her until 1778 as a receiving ship, and she was broken up in 1800.
*Odysseus* is a 1980 role-playing game set in ancient Greece.

Charlotte Deane MBE (born May 6, 1975) is a Professor of Structural Bioinformatics at the University of Oxford and Deputy Executive Chair of the Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council (EPSRC).

Joseph Holston (born 1944) is an American painter and printmaker known for his bold use of color and expressive forms. His work, including the series *Color in Freedom: Journey along the Underground Railroad*, explores African American history and identity. His art is held in major collections, including The Phillips Collection and the Smithsonian American Art Museum.

MyPertamina is a loyalty program by PT Pertamina, an Indonesian state-owned oil and gas company. It began in December 2016 as merchandise outlets, and in August 2017, expanded to include a mobile app and electronic card.

Scott L. Thoele is a United States Army officer and veteran of the Gulf War, Iraq War, and War in Afghanistan. Commissioned in 1980, he served with the 82nd Airborne Division, 12th Special Forces Group, and later commanded in the 20th Special Forces Group. His decorations include the Legion of Merit, Bronze Star, and Combat Infantryman Badge. As a civilian, he works as a vice president and credit analyst at First Bankers Trust Company in Quincy, Illinois. He holds degrees from Quincy University and the University of Wisconsin-Madison.
Joseph McLaughlin was an Irish-American politician and Republican congressman from Pennsylvania, serving in the Sixty-fifth and Sixty-seventh Congresses.

First Manhattan Co. (FMC) is a financial services and asset management firm founded in 1964 by David Gottesman and headquartered in New York City. The firm manages over $16 billion in assets (as of 2013) for high-net-worth individuals, trusts, retirement accounts, and institutional clients, employing a long-term, value-oriented investment strategy. Known for its ties to Berkshire Hathaway and Warren Buffett, FMC focuses on fundamental analysis and U.S. equity and fixed-income markets. The firm is led by CEO Robert Gottesman and a management committee. In 2013, FMC played a prominent role in a proxy contest to restructure the leadership of biotech company VIVUS, Inc.

Nippu Ravva is a 1993 Indian Telugu-language film directed by A. Kodandarami Reddy and produced by M. V. Srinivas Prasad. The film stars Nandamuri Balakrishna and Vijayashanti, with music composed by Bappi Lahiri and Raj–Koti. The plot follows Suryam, a trade union leader wrongfully imprisoned for murder, who escapes and abducts sports star Vijaya. As he fights against the oppressive mine owner Dhanraj, Suryam's struggle escalates into a nationwide movement, culminating in a fierce confrontation and a tragic conclusion.

"I'd Go to Jail" is a 2021 country song by Canadian artist Dean Brody, released as a single from his album *Boys*. Inspired by fatherhood, the song explores themes of love and protection. It reached No. 5 on *Billboard* Canada Country and No. 73 on the *Canadian Hot 100*.
S. P. Singh (born 1948) is an Indian biochemist, professor, and author.

The Coaldale Big Green was a professional American football team based in Coaldale, Pennsylvania, founded in 1912 and active until approximately 1933. Initially known as Coaldale Football until 1917, the team adopted the "Big Green" name after donning green jerseys. Managed by James H. Gildea, the team primarily featured local talent and achieved notable success, winning the Curran Cup in 1921, 1922, and 1923. The Big Green played in various leagues, including the Anthracite League and the Eastern League of Professional Football, but faced challenges in the late 1920s, ultimately becoming a traveling team before folding.
Jamil Baroody (1905 – death date unknown) was a Lebanese diplomat. A representative of Saudi Arabia to the United Nations from its founding, he was known for his oratory.
South Venice is a census-designated place (CDP) in Sarasota County, Florida, with a population of 15,619 as of 2020. It spans 6.34 square miles and features a predominantly residential community.

The Captain Benjamin Williams House is a historic Georgian-style home in Middletown, Connecticut, built around 1797. Located at 27 Washington Street, it features brick construction, brownstone detailing, and a hip roof with dormers. Originally owned by sea captain Benjamin Williams, it later belonged to the deKoven family before being restored in the 20th century. The house was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1978 and is part of the Middletown Heritage Trail.

Club Deportivo Arturo Fernández Vial, commonly known as Fernández Vial, is a Chilean football club based in Concepción. Founded in 1903, the club is named after Admiral Arturo Fernández Vial, who mediated a labor dispute that inspired its creation. Nicknamed *La Máquina Aurinegra* ("The Yellow-and-Black Machine"), the club rose from Chile's third division to the top tier in just two years (1981–1982), a feat unmatched in Chilean football. Currently competing in the Primera B, the club plays at the Estadio Municipal de Concepción.

Rishta Ho To Aisa (1992) is a Hindi drama film directed by Kalptaru, starring Jeetendra, Rishi Kapoor, Juhi Chawla, and Sujata Mehta. The story follows a man who loses his memory, leading to mistaken identities and family conflicts before a dramatic reunion. The film features music by Laxmikant–Pyarelal.

WNSN (101.5 FM), branded as "Sunny 101.5," is a commercial adult contemporary radio station licensed to South Bend, Indiana, and serving the Michiana area. The station first signed on as WSBT-FM on August 1, 1962, initially simulcasting its AM counterpart before transitioning to beautiful music and later adopting its current adult contemporary format in 1984 under the call sign WNSN. Owned by Mid-West Family Broadcasting, WNSN operates alongside sister stations WSBT, WQLQ, and WZOC. The station has undergone several ownership changes, including its 2016 acquisition by Mid-West Family Broadcasting following the sale of its previous owner, Schurz Communications, to Gray Television.

Santiago de Cuba railway station, officially named Santiago de Cuba General Senén Casas, is a major railway terminus located in Santiago de Cuba, Cuba. Rebuilt in 1997, the station features a modern structure with four platforms and eight tracks, serving as a key hub for long-distance trains across the island, including the flagship Tren Francés. It connects Santiago to various cities, including Havana, Guantánamo, and Bayamo, and is accessible to passengers.
Nicholas N. Cox (1837–1912) was a Democratic U.S. Congressman from Tennessee's 7th district, serving from 1891 to 1901, and a Confederate Army colonel during the Civil War.

Eugène-René Poubelle (15 April 1831 – 15 July 1907) was a French lawyer, university lecturer, and government administrator, best known for introducing the dustbin, or "poubelle," to France during his tenure as préfet of the Seine. His 1884 decree mandated waste sorting and collection in Paris, revolutionizing urban sanitation. Poubelle also championed direct sewer connections to combat cholera. Later, he served as ambassador to the Vatican and advocated for Southern France's wine industry. His legacy includes the widespread use of the word "poubelle" for trash bins and commemorations such as a Parisian street and a Google Doodle celebrating his 190th birthday.

Declo is a city in Cassia County, Idaho, with a population of 343 as of the 2010 census. Originally named Marshfield, it was renamed Declo in 1916. The city is known for its semi-arid climate and is part of the Cassia County School District.
Masʿūd II of Ṭur ʿAbdin (fl. 15th–16th century) was a Syriac Orthodox patriarch and bishop, known for his monastic reforms and controversial leadership.

Koforidua Senior High Technical School (KSTS) is a secondary technical school in Koforidua, Ghana, established in 1967 by Dr. Kwame Nkrumah. Initially an all-boys school, it began admitting girls in 1991. Known for its strong academic performance, KSTS is ranked among Ghana's Class A schools and achieved notable success in the West African Senior School Certificate Examination (WASSCE).
Manilal Gandhi (1892–1956) was the second son of Mohandas Gandhi, who followed in his father's footsteps as an activist.

Down High School is a grammar school in Downpatrick, County Down, Northern Ireland, established in 1933. The school serves approximately 1,080 students aged 11 to 18 and is situated on a rural campus. Its motto, *Floreat Dunum, Absque Labore Nihil* ("May Down flourish, nothing is achieved without effort"), reflects its commitment to academic and personal growth. The school’s crest, inspired by the badge of 12th-century Norman soldier John de Courcy, symbolizes its historical ties to the region. Notable features of the campus include a gatehouse and stone walls, remnants of the former county gaol, and five student houses fostering community spirit.

Caledonite is a rare sulfate mineral with the formula Cu₂Pb₅(OH)₆CO₃(SO₄)₃, characterized by its blue-green color and vitreous luster. It typically forms striated prismatic or tabular crystals and is primarily valued as a collector's mineral rather than an ore.

Jackson Township is one of thirty-seven Jackson Townships located in the state of Ohio. Located in Richland County, it borders several other townships. Part of the city of Shelby is located in Jackson Township. The township is governed by a three-member board of trustees.

Walter G. Alexander (1880–1953) was an American physician and politician. He was the first African American to serve in the New Jersey Legislature, elected to the General Assembly in 1920. He was also active in the NAACP and the National Medical Association.
Eleanor Perenyi (born Eleanor Spencer Stone; January 4, 1918 – May 3, 2009) was an American author. She is best known for *Green Thoughts: A Writer in the Garden* and her memoir *More Was Lost*.

*1939 Battle of Westerplatte* is a 2013 Polish-Lithuanian war film directed by Paweł Chochlew, depicting the Battle of Westerplatte during the Nazi invasion of Poland. The film sparked controversy for its perceived revisionist portrayal of the battle, with some critics labeling it "anti-Polish" and offensive to the honor of Polish soldiers. Despite this, others praised it as a tribute to the defenders' heroism. The film's title and marketing underwent changes to address criticism.

Henry Monsang Tesong (born 28 February 1994) is an Indian footballer from Manipur who plays as a defender for Sagolband United. He previously played for Pune and Chennai City in the I-League, making his professional debut with Chennai City in 2017.

Ranpak is an American packaging company specializing in recyclable paper-based cushioning and void-fill solutions. Founded in 1972 in Painesville, Ohio, it produces machines that convert kraft paper into protective packaging materials. Ranpak operates globally, with manufacturing in North America, Europe, and Asia. The company has undergone multiple ownership changes, including acquisitions by private equity firms, and went public in 2019. Recognized for innovation, it was named among *Fast Company*'s top logistics innovators in 2021 for its sustainable alternatives to plastic packaging. Ranpak is headquartered in Concord, Ohio.

The February 2016 Ankara bombing was a car bombing and suicide attack that occurred on February 17, 2016, in Ankara, Turkey, targeting military personnel. The explosion, which took place near military buses waiting at a traffic light, killed 30 people, including 14 soldiers, 14 civilian employees of the Turkish Armed Forces, one civilian, and the perpetrator, and injured 60 others. The Kurdistan Freedom Hawks (TAK) claimed responsibility for the attack, identifying the bomber as Abdulbaki Sömer, a Turkish citizen, though initial government reports had incorrectly named a Syrian Kurd as the assailant.

The bombing occurred amidst heightened tensions in the Kurdish–Turkish conflict and ongoing Turkish military actions against Kurdish groups. The attack drew widespread condemnation domestically and internationally, with Turkish leaders vowing retaliation. The incident also intensified debates over Turkey's policies toward Kurdish groups and its relations with NATO allies.

"Killing Birds" is a 1988 Italian horror film directed by Claudio Lattanzi and produced by Aristide Massaccesi. The plot follows Fred Brown, a soldier who returns from the Vietnam War to find his wife with another man, leading him to commit murder before being attacked by a falcon that blinds him. Twenty years later, a group of college seniors searching for a rare bird encounters supernatural occurrences linked to Brown's past. The film features a mix of horror and zombie elements, with a narrative that explores themes of fear and survival. It was released in France and Italy, with variations in gore content for different markets.

Birna Arnbjörnsdóttir (born 1952) is an Icelandic linguist and professor specializing in second-language acquisition. She has contributed extensively to research on Icelandic as a heritage language, language contact, and the influence of English in Iceland. A professor at the University of Iceland since 2000, she has led numerous research projects, including the development of Icelandic Online, a widely recognized free language-learning platform. Birna has also been active in international research networks and language technology initiatives. She has served in various academic and community roles, including as Chair of the Vigdís Finnbogadóttir Institute of Foreign Languages.

Trabzonspor Kulübü Kadın Futbol Takımı is the women's football team of Trabzonspor, based in Trabzon, Turkey. Founded in 2007, the team won the Turkish Women's Football League in the 2008–09 season, becoming the first Turkish club to win both men's and women's premier league titles. Re-established in 2021, they currently compete in the Turkish Women's Football Super League. The team also participated in the 2009–10 UEFA Women's Champions League qualifiers.
The women's 1500 metres event at the 2016 Summer Olympics took place between 12 and 16 August 2016 at the Olympic Stadium.

Sir Peter Edward Bairsto (3 August 1926 – 24 October 2017) was a senior Royal Air Force officer who served as Deputy Commander of Strike Command (1981–84). A decorated veteran, he was awarded the Air Force Cross and twice received the Queen's Commendation for Valuable Service in the Air.

Cóndores UNAM was an American football team representing the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), active from 1970 to 1997. Competing in ONEFA, they won 10 national championships, dominating college football in Mexico under coach Diego García Miravete. The team was dissolved in 1998 when UNAM merged its football programs under the Pumas name.
Boubacar Moungoro (born September 22, 1994) is a Malian professional basketball player, currently a free agent, who has played as a shooting guard and small forward.

The murder of Lieth Von Stein occurred on July 25, 1988, in Washington, North Carolina, when he and his wife, Bonnie, were attacked in their home. Lieth was killed, and Bonnie survived with serious injuries. The investigation revealed that Lieth's stepson, Chris Pritchard, had orchestrated the crime, enlisting his college friends Neal Henderson and James Upchurch to carry out the attack in hopes of inheriting part of Lieth's $2 million estate. All three were convicted, with Upchurch receiving a life sentence, Pritchard serving time before being paroled in 2007, and Henderson paroled in 2000.

The case gained national attention and was the subject of two books, *Cruel Doubt* and *Blood Games*, as well as television adaptations. These works controversially linked the crime to the perpetrators' interest in Dungeons & Dragons, a connection that critics have called tenuous.

XBase++ is a Clipper-syntax compiler created after Computer Associates abandoned Clipper. It supports the Replaceable Database Drivers (RDD) of Clipper through DatabaseEngines (DBEs), including DBF, FOX, NTX, CDX, SDF, and DEL. XBase++ also supports the @SAY/GET commands, a graphic editor, a visual development environment, an integrated debugger, and a resource compiler. It can generate EXE or DLL files.

Fungistatics are substances that prevent fungi from growing. They are used in both medicine, such as fluconazole and itraconazole, to treat fungal infections, and as food preservatives, such as sodium benzoate and potassium sorbate.

Lead Section for *Jumping Flash! 2*  

*Jumping Flash! 2* is a 1996 first-person platformer developed by Exact and MuuMuu and published by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation. A sequel to *Jumping Flash!* (1995), the game follows Robbit, a robotic rabbit, as he battles the villainous Captain Kabuki and Baron Aloha to save the planet Little Muu. Retaining the original’s core mechanics, players navigate 3D environments using triple jumps while shooting enemies and collecting power-ups. New features include expanded levels, underwater segments, rideable objects, and Performance Medals awarded for completing challenges.  

Critics praised its improved graphics, larger worlds, and creative additions but noted its short length and low difficulty. The game was re-released on PlayStation Network in 2009. While some reviewers considered it more of an expansion than a full sequel, it was regarded as a worthy follow-up that refined the original’s formula. *Electronic Gaming Monthly* ranked it among the "100 Best Games of All Time" in 1997.
Judith Révész (1915–2018) was a Hungarian-Dutch ceramicist and sculptor known for her earthenware and bronze works.
Francisco Fervenza Fernández (1907–1998) was a Spanish anarchist and military commander during the Spanish Civil War.
Liverpool won the 1965–66 Football League First Division title.

Lead Section:  

*Hadith: A Re-evaluation* is a 1986 book by Malaysian scholar Kassim Ahmed, arguing that the Quran alone is Islam's authoritative source and that hadith are human inventions prone to contradictions. The book claims that relying on hadith for religious law is an illegitimate innovation (*bid‘ah*). It was banned in Malaysia, and Ahmed's lectures on the topic were canceled. Critics, including M. A. Rauf, defended hadith as essential to Islamic tradition. (63 words)

The inSpiral Lounge was a vegetarian and vegan café, music venue, and community space in Camden, London, operating from 2007 to 2016. Known for its focus on sustainability, art, and ambient music, it hosted performances by notable artists, supported local artists, and participated in festivals like Glastonbury. It also ran a bakery, online shop, and wholesale business, and was involved in environmental initiatives such as London's Capital Growth Project. The venue received multiple awards, including recognition as one of London's top vegetarian restaurants.

Fungistatics are substances that inhibit the growth of fungi. Common examples include antifungal medications like fluconazole and itraconazole, as well as food preservatives such as sodium benzoate and potassium sorbate, which prevent fungal contamination in food and beverages.

Annie Buller (December 9, 1895 – January 19, 1973) was a Canadian labor organizer, political activist, and founding member of the Communist Party of Canada. Known for her union organizing and advocacy for workers' rights, she played a key role in strikes and labor movements across Canada, including the 1931 Toronto dressmakers' strike and the Estevan Coal Miners Strike. Buller was imprisoned for her activism and later became a prominent figure in the Labor-Progressive Party. She remained active in political organizing, anti-war efforts, and women's campaigns until her retirement in the late 1950s.

StarUML is a proprietary UML modeling tool developed by MKLabs, supporting UML 2.0, SysML, and legacy notations. Initially released in 2006 as open-source, it transitioned to a commercial model in 2014. It runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

Rod Diridon Sr. (born February 8, 1939) is an American transportation advocate and former elected official. A retired U.S. Navy officer, he served on the Santa Clara County Board of Supervisors (1975–1995) and chaired multiple regional transit agencies. Known as the "father of modern transit" in Silicon Valley, he led efforts to establish light rail, CalTrain, and the renaming of San Jose's Diridon Station in his honor. He later chaired the California High-Speed Rail Authority (2001–2003) and co-founded the Mineta Transportation Institute, where he served as executive director (1993–2014). A longtime civic leader, he has also been active in environmental, educational, and philanthropic organizations.

CollabNet, founded in 1999 by Tim O'Reilly, Brian Behlendorf, and Bill Portelli, is a private software company headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia. It specializes in business-to-business software solutions for global enterprises and government organizations, focusing on agile management, DevOps, and value stream management. The company merged with VersionOne in 2017 and has since expanded its offerings in software development and management.

Bobby Beck is the CEO and President of Animation Mentor, an online animation school headquartered in Emeryville, California. Prior to founding Animation Mentor in 2004, Beck worked as an animator at Pixar, where he contributed to films such as *Toy Story 2*, *Monsters, Inc.*, *Finding Nemo*, *The Incredibles*, and *Cars*. He also worked at Walt Disney Feature Animation and Tippet Studios.

Matthew Asen Kantakouzenos (c. 1325 – 1383 or 1391) was a Byzantine emperor who reigned from 1353 to 1357 during a period of civil war. The son of Emperor John VI Kantakouzenos, he was granted Thrace as an appanage and later proclaimed co-emperor. His reign was marked by conflicts with John V Palaiologos and military campaigns against the Serbs, culminating in his capture and forced abdication. Afterward, he governed the Morea from 1380 to 1383 before ceding power to his son, Demetrios I Kantakouzenos.

Hopatcong State Park is a 163-acre (0.66 km²) public recreation area located in Roxbury Township, New Jersey, on the shores of Lake Hopatcong. Established in 1922, the park offers swimming, fishing, boating, and winter activities like ice fishing and sledding. It features picnic areas, playgrounds, sports courts, and a boat ramp. The park also preserves historical elements, including the Lake Hopatcong Historical Museum, housed in a former Morris Canal lock tender’s house, and a display of canal technology, including a Scotch Turbine from the Morris Canal. Operated by the New Jersey Division of Parks and Forestry, it is a popular destination for outdoor recreation.

Hilal is an elevated rapid transit station on the İzmir Metro, located in the Ege Mahallesi of Konak, İzmir. Opened on May 22, 2000, it serves the M1 line and features one island platform with two tracks. The station is accessible and connects to the İZBAN at Hilal, facilitating transfers to the regional rail network. The station's design includes a mezzanine level for fare control and ticketing, with walkways and bridges providing access to surrounding streets and neighborhoods. Hilal is one of only two elevated stations on the İzmir Metro.

Donald Aronow (March 1, 1927 – February 3, 1987) was an American boat designer, builder, and racer, known for founding several successful powerboat companies, including Donzi Marine, Magnum Marine, and Cigarette Racing Team. A two-time world champion and three-time U.S. champion, his boats won over 350 offshore races and were used by the U.S. Customs Service. Aronow was murdered in 1987 in a case linked to business disputes and drug smuggling.

Ward No. 8 of the Kolkata Municipal Corporation covers the neighborhoods of Bagbazar and Shobhabazar in Kolkata, West Bengal, India. It is part of Borough No. 1 and falls under the Shyampukur Assembly constituency and Kolkata Uttar parliamentary constituency. As of the 2011 Census, the ward had a population of 18,760 with a literacy rate of 93.57%. It is bordered by the Hooghly River and several streets, and is served by Shyampukur police station. The ward forms a municipal corporation council electoral constituency.

"The Romance of Tiger and Rose" (also known as "The Rumored Chen Qianqian") is a Chinese romantic drama and comedy series created and written by Nan Zhen, directed by Cha Chuanyi, and starring Zhao Lusi and Ding Yu Xi. The show follows Chen Xiaoqian, a screenwriter who, after a nap, finds herself transported into her own script as the doomed third princess, Chen Qianqian. To survive and advance the plot, she must navigate her new reality and bring the lead characters together. The series aired on Tencent from May 18 to June 1, 2020, and has received positive reviews for its unique premise.

Fatima Bhutto (born May 29, 1982) is a Pakistani writer, columnist, and member of the prominent Bhutto political family. Born in Kabul, Afghanistan, to Murtaza Bhutto and Fauzia Fasihudin, she grew up stateless, moving frequently with her father after her parents' divorce. Bhutto is the granddaughter of former Pakistani Prime Minister Zulfikar Ali Bhutto and niece of Benazir Bhutto. She was educated at Barnard College, Columbia University, and SOAS, University of London, where she studied Middle Eastern and South Asian cultures.

Bhutto has published several works, including the memoir *Songs of Blood and Sword* (2010) and novels such as *The Shadow of the Crescent Moon* (2013) and *The Runaways* (2019). While politically active, she has chosen to focus on writing and activism rather than pursuing elected office, citing concerns about dynastic politics in Pakistan.

Dan Carpenter (born November 25, 1985) is an American former placekicker. He played college football at Montana, and professionally for the Miami Dolphins (2008-2012) and Buffalo Bills (2013-2016). Carpenter was named to the Pro Bowl in 2009.

*Twentieth Century* is the twenty-first studio album by American country music band Alabama, released on June 15, 1999, by RCA Nashville. Produced by Alabama and Don Cook, the album was recorded at Soundshop Recording Studios in Nashville, Tennessee. While praised for its polished production and harmonies, the album received mixed reviews, with critics noting its lack of distinctiveness compared to the band's earlier work. Stephen Thomas Erlewine of *AllMusic* rated it 2 out of 5 stars, highlighting tracks like "Twentieth Century" and "God Must Have Spent a Little More Time on You" as standouts but criticizing the album for blending into a "pleasant but forgettable" sound. Notably, drummer Mark Herndon is uncredited on the album.

Stephen Langton (c. 1150 – 9 July 1228) was an English Cardinal of the Roman Catholic Church and Archbishop of Canterbury. His appointment as archbishop led to a bitter dispute between King John of England and Pope Innocent III. Langton is widely credited with dividing the Bible into the standard modern arrangement of chapters.

Shikantaza, a term derived from the Sino-Japanese reading of Zhǐguǎn dǎzuò, translates to "just sitting" and refers to a form of meditation primarily associated with Zen Buddhism. It emphasizes wholehearted engagement in zazen, or seated meditation, and is characterized by silent illumination, a practice articulated by the Caodong school of Chan. Dōgen, a key figure in Zen, used shikantaza to denote a state of open awareness and alertness during meditation, reflecting the reality of life without distraction or drowsiness.

The Taylorcraft Auster was a British light aircraft developed from the American Taylorcraft Model A, adapted for military use as an Air Observation Post (AOP) during World War II. It was widely used by British, Canadian, and Polish forces for artillery spotting and liaison duties. Post-war variants continued in service until replaced by helicopters.

The Azerbaijan Carpet Museum, established in 1967 and located in Baku, showcases a vast collection of over 10,000 items, including carpets, ceramics, and metal works. Initially housed in the Juma Mosque, the museum moved to a new building designed to resemble a rolled carpet, which opened in 2014. The museum also organizes international exhibitions and educational programs, highlighting the cultural significance of Azerbaijani carpet art.

Leslie Harrison Lambert (1883–1941), known as A. J. Alan, was a British magician, intelligence officer, author, and radio broadcaster. As A. J. Alan, he became a popular BBC radio personality known for his seemingly extemporaneous short stories.

Levon Zekiyan is an Armenian archbishop and scholar, known for his contributions to Armenian studies, philosophy, and theology. He graduated from the Mekhitarist Seminary in 1959 and later earned master's degrees in Philosophy and Theology from the Pontificia Università Gregoriana in Rome. Zekiyan completed his PhD at the State University of Istanbul in 1973. He served as Principal of Moorat-Raphael College from 1982 to 1985 and was Secretary of the Armenian Academy of the Mekhitarist Fathers in Venice from 1974 to 1981. In 2014, he was appointed apostolic administrator of the archieparchy of Istanbul of the Armenians by Pope Francis.

Lashkargah (Pashto/Dari: لشکرگاه), also spelled Lashkar Gah and formerly called Bost, is a city in southwestern Afghanistan and the capital of Helmand Province. Situated along the Helmand River, it has a population of approximately 276,831 as of 2015.

Founded as a riverside barracks town for soldiers of the Ghaznavid dynasty in the 9th century, Lashkargah served as their winter capital. The city was later rebuilt by Timur after being sacked by various empires. In the 20th century, it was redeveloped by the United States Army Corps of Engineers as the headquarters for the Helmand Valley Authority (HVA) irrigation project, modeled after the Tennessee Valley Authority.

Lashkargah has a hot desert climate with little precipitation. The Helmand River provides opportunities for recreation, and the city is home to historical sites such as the Ghaznavid fortress of Lashkari Bazar and the Qala-e-Bost. The city has undergone development in recent years, with improvements to infrastructure and services. Football and cricket are popular sports in the area. After heavy fighting, the city fell under Taliban control on 13 August 2021.

The POW/MIA flag is a symbol of the United States' commitment to resolving the fates of American prisoners of war (POWs) and those missing in action (MIA). Designed in 1971 by Newt Heisley for the National League of Families, the flag features a silhouette of a POW, a guard tower, and barbed wire, with the words "POW/MIA" and "You Are Not Forgotten." Officially recognized by Congress in 1990, it is flown on federal properties and military sites to honor and remember POW/MIAs from all U.S. conflicts.

San José Castle (Spanish: *Castillo de San José*), also known as Santa Bárbara Fort, is a 19th-century military fortress in Guatemala City, Guatemala. Built between 1843 and 1846 under President Rafael Carrera, it served as a strategic defense post and later as an ammunition depot. The fort played a key role in the 1920 revolution that ousted President Manuel Estrada Cabrera, when it was a major stronghold before falling to rebel forces. Today, it houses the Museo del Ejército (Army Museum).

Olwen Williams is a Welsh physician and healthcare leader, currently serving as the RCP Vice President for Wales. She has held various prominent positions, including Vice-President of the Medical Women's Federation and clinical lead for the Royal College of Physicians' Future Hospitals project.

Trochilinae is a subfamily of hummingbirds (family Trochilidae). A molecular phylogenetic study divided the hummingbird family into multiple subfamilies and redefined the Trochilinae to contain three tribes: Lampornithini, Mellisugini, and Trochilini.

The Aeroprakt A-22 Foxbat is a Ukrainian two-seat, high-wing, single-engine ultralight aircraft designed by Aeroprakt in 1990, with the first prototype flying in 1996. A German-certified version entered production in 1999.

The A-22 is primarily metal in construction, with fabric-covered wings and control surfaces. It features extensive glazing for excellent visibility, including convex doors. It utilizes a 3-axis control system and flaperons, resulting in a low stall speed. The aircraft is typically powered by a Rotax 912UL or 912ULS engine, with the Jabiru 2200 also being an option.

Stepan Petrovich Krasheninnikov (November 11, 1711 – March 8, 1755) was a Russian naturalist and geographer known for his explorations of Kamchatka and Siberia during the Second Kamchatka Expedition (1731–42). Educated at the Slavic Greek Latin Academy, he made significant contributions to natural history, ethnology, and linguistics, publishing his findings in 1755. Krasheninnikov also served as a professor and head of the Academy's Botanic Garden.

In Latin grammar, the supine is a verbal noun with two forms. The first supine, ending in -tum, indicates purpose with verbs of motion or fate with the future passive infinitive. The second supine, ending in -ū, is rare and used with adjectives. Similar forms exist in Sanskrit, Germanic, Finnic, Romance, Slavic, and Baltic languages.

"Lottery" is a short story by Indian author Premchand, published in 1933. Narrated from the perspective of a school teacher and his friend Vikram, the story explores their discussions about winning a lottery worth ten lakhs of Indian rupees. As they pool their resources to buy a ticket, family tensions rise, culminating in a twist ending that delivers a moral message.
Taiwanese legislative election in 1992: KMT won 95 of 161 seats.

Lou Rinaldi (born 1947) is a former Canadian politician and businessman who served as a Liberal Member of Provincial Parliament (MPP) in Ontario. Representing Northumberland and later Northumberland—Quinte West, he held office from 2003 to 2011 and 2014 to 2018. Rinaldi also served as Parliamentary Assistant to various ministers during his tenure. Before entering provincial politics, he was active in municipal government, serving as mayor of Brighton, Ontario, where he oversaw the establishment of the Brighton Health Services Centre. Originally from Patrica, Italy, Rinaldi immigrated to Canada in 1960 and operated a small business in the automotive industry.

Glory Lefoshie Sedibe (May 16, 1953 – March 20, 1994), also known by aliases such as Comrade September, was a South African anti-apartheid activist, intelligence operative, and later an askari (enemy agent). Born in Pilgrim's Rest, Mpumalanga, Sedibe joined the African National Congress (ANC) and its military wing, Umkhonto we Sizwe, in 1977, rising to become head of intelligence for the Transvaal region after receiving training in East Germany and the Soviet Union. 

In 1986, Sedibe was abducted by apartheid police in Swaziland with the assistance of local authorities. Following months of torture, he became an askari, collaborating with the apartheid regime to capture and kill his former ANC comrades. He later served as a key state witness in the 1988 treason trial of anti-apartheid activist Ebrahim Ebrahim and others. Sedibe died under mysterious circumstances in 1994, with conflicting accounts attributing his death to poisoning or heart failure. His legacy remains controversial due to his defection and actions as an askari.

*DRUM*, originally titled *African Drum*, was a South African magazine founded in 1951. Initially, it offered a paternalistic view of Africans but soon shifted its focus to the vibrant urban Black townships. During its heyday in the 1950s, *DRUM* chronicled the culture, dreams, and struggles of urban Black life amidst apartheid. The magazine fostered a talented group of Black journalists and photographers, including Henry Nxumalo, Jürgen Schadeberg and Peter Magubane. *DRUM* ceased print publication in July 2020.

NBA Street Vol. 2 is a 2003 arcade-style basketball video game developed by NuFX and EA Canada, and published by EA Sports BIG. A sequel to *NBA Street*, it features 3-on-3 street basketball with NBA teams and legends, including three versions of Michael Jordan. Players compete in modes such as *Be a Legend*, *NBA Challenge*, and *Pick Up Game*, using trick moves and "Gamebreaker" abilities to gain advantages. The game includes a hip-hop soundtrack with artists like Nelly and Nate Dogg. It received critical acclaim, with praise for its gameplay, presentation, and multiplayer experience.

The 500-series format is a modular audio equipment standard developed by Automated Processes, Inc. (API) in 1969, based on designs by Saul Walker and Lou Lindauer. Originally used in API mixing consoles, the format allows for interchangeable preamps, equalizers, and compressors in portable chassis, such as the API Lunchbox. The VPR Alliance, formed in 2006, standardized specifications for voltage, dimensions, and pin configurations to ensure compatibility. By 2020, over 500 different modules were available from API and third-party manufacturers, including Neve, Solid State Logic, and dbx, making the 500-series a widely adopted format in professional audio.
Chuck Wichgers (born 1965) is an American politician and Republican member of the Wisconsin State Assembly.

Dardanella was a prominent toneel (theatre) troupe in the Dutch East Indies (now Indonesia), active from 1926 to 1936. Founded by Willy A. Piedro, it adapted Hollywood films and original stories into Malay-language productions, blending European theatrical styles with local themes. The troupe starred actors like Devi Dja and Tan Tjeng Bok, earning acclaim for its performances. Later joined by playwright Andjar Asmara, Dardanella shifted toward more serious dramas. Though it disbanded after a failed film project, its members influenced early Indonesian cinema. The troupe was praised for promoting national unity through Malay and diverse cultural representation.

New areas (Chinese: 新区) in the People's Republic of China are special economic development zones established to promote economic growth and attract investment. State-level new areas are supported by the central government and granted preferential policies by the State Council, while provincial- and prefectural-level new areas are managed by local governments.

*The Art of Self-Defense* is a 2019 black comedy film directed and written by Riley Stearns, starring Jesse Eisenberg, Alessandro Nivola, and Imogen Poots. The film follows timid accountant Casey, who, after being attacked, joins a karate dōjō led by the charismatic Sensei. As he becomes entrenched in the dōjō's toxic culture, Casey's journey explores themes of masculinity and violence, culminating in a dark confrontation with Sensei. The film premiered at South by Southwest and received generally favorable reviews, praised for its unique blend of humor and thriller elements.

*Uncle Jam Wants You* is the eleventh studio album by the American funk band Funkadelic, released on September 21, 1979, by Warner Bros. Records. Produced by George Clinton, the album serves as a more militant sequel to *One Nation Under a Groove* (1978), aiming to "rescue dance music from the blahs" and provoke a cultural shift into Funkadelia. Its cover art, featuring Clinton in a Huey Newton-inspired pose, reflects the album's martial themes. The record includes Funkadelic's last major hit, "(Not Just) Knee Deep," which topped the Billboard Soul singles chart and became highly influential in West Coast hip-hop, inspiring artists like De La Soul and Dr. Dre.
**Uma Devi Khatri** (11 July 1923 – 24 November 2003), better known as **Tun Tun**, was an Indian playback singer, actress, and comedian in Bollywood films, active from 1946 to 1990.

Marco Pinotti (born February 25, 1976) is a retired Italian professional road cyclist known for his expertise in time trials. Nicknamed "L'Ingegnere" (The Engineer) and "Il Professore" (The Professor), he achieved significant success, including two individual stage wins at the Giro d'Italia and multiple national time trial championships.
The 1960 Spanish Guinea by-election elected six members to the Spanish parliament's Cortes Españolas.

The Koza riot was a violent and spontaneous protest against the U.S. military presence in Okinawa, Japan, which occurred on December 20, 1970. Triggered by a traffic accident involving an American serviceman, the riot involved thousands of Okinawan civilians who damaged property and injured American military personnel. The riot reflected long-standing tensions and discontent over the U.S. military occupation of Okinawa, which, while soon to end, was seen as unjust due to incidents and legal exemptions for U.S. service members.
Gretta Kehoe-Quigley is a former Irish camogie player from Wexford who played as a center back. She won a Leinster senior championship in 1974 and an All-Ireland medal in 1975 alongside her sisters.

The Black sermonic tradition is a distinctive style of preaching in African American Christianity, characterized by emotional expressiveness, rhetorical embellishments, and musical vocalizations. It emphasizes authenticity, social critique, and spiritual transformation while maintaining historical ties to ancestral struggles. A notable feature is "whooping," a dynamic call-and-response style often accompanied by organ chords. The tradition has also influenced African American literature and poetry.

Kazimieras Jaunius (19 May 1848 – 9 March 1908) was a Lithuanian Catholic priest, linguist, and professor, best known for his contributions to the study of the Lithuanian language. Born into a peasant family in Kovno Governorate, he studied at the Kaunas Priest Seminary and later at the Saint Petersburg Roman Catholic Theological Academy, where he mastered multiple languages.  

Jaunius taught at the Kaunas Priest Seminary, inspiring future leaders of the Lithuanian National Revival, including Maironis. His work on Lithuanian grammar, though incomplete, became foundational for later linguists like Jonas Jablonskis. He also classified Lithuanian dialects, establishing a framework later refined by Antanas Salys. Despite mental health struggles, he continued linguistic research, advising the Russian government on Lithuanian script reforms before the 1904 ban lift.  

Appointed professor in Saint Petersburg, he mentored Kazimieras Būga, who posthumously published his works. Jaunius died in poverty, but his legacy endured through memorials and his influence on Lithuanian linguistics.
The Council on Religion and the Homosexual (CRH) was formed in 1964 to promote dialogue between religious communities and the LGBT community.

Shaarey Zedek Cemetery is a Jewish cemetery in Winnipeg, Manitoba, established in 1894 and owned by Shaarey Zedek Synagogue. The largest Jewish cemetery in the Canadian Prairies, it contains over 8,000 graves, including those of prominent rabbis and the Bronfman family. It features two ohelim (mausoleums), Commonwealth war graves, and a cenotaph honoring Jewish soldiers who died in both World Wars. In 2012, an interfaith burial section, Shaarey Shamayim Cemetery, was added. The cemetery has been the site of historic events, including a "black wedding" during the 1918 flu pandemic, and has faced vandalism in the past.

"Up the Elephant and Round the Castle" is a British comedy sitcom that aired on ITV from November 30, 1983, to November 7, 1985. Created by Colin Bostock-Smith, Jim Eldridge, Tony Hoare, and Spike Mullins, the show follows Jim London, a charming rogue who inherits a dilapidated property in Elephant and Castle, leading to various misadventures. The series consists of three seasons and 22 episodes.

Ninnes is a locality in South Australia, located 16 km west of Lochiel, with a population of 65 as of 2021. Established in the 1860s, it was part of the Hundred of Ninnes proclaimed in 1874. The District Council of Ninnes was formed in 1885, later renamed Bute in 1933.

Ben James Herbert Burridge (11 March 1898 – 22 December 1977) was an English footballer and manager who played as a half-back. He had a lengthy career spanning both league and non-league football, including stints with Darlington, Sheffield Wednesday, Oldham Athletic, and several Cheshire League clubs. Burridge captained Macclesfield to consecutive Cheshire County League titles and later served as player-manager for Hurst. He died in 1977, aged 79.

Europe is a station on Line 3 of the Paris Métro, located in the 8th arrondissement of Paris. Opened on 19 October 1904, it is named after Place de l'Europe, a square from which streets named for European capitals radiate. The station underwent renovations in 2000 and was culturally developed for the metro's centenary. In 2018, it was renamed Europe - Simone-Veil to honor Simone Veil, a prominent French politician and Holocaust survivor. The station features a Guimard entrance, designated a historical monument, and is served by several RATP bus and Noctilien night bus lines.

JD Eicher is an American singer, songwriter, producer, and novelist from Youngstown, Ohio, known for his contributions to the alt-pop and indie music genres. He began playing guitar at age 12 and formed the band JD Eicher & the Goodnights while studying at Westminster College. Eicher has released several albums, including a trilogy that explores themes of life and change. Notably, he provided an EP soundtrack for Nicholas Sparks' novel *Two by Two*, which featured the original song "Two by Two." Eicher has performed at various venues and festivals, opened for major artists, and created the annual JD's Summer Songfest to benefit local charities. In 2021, he signed with AntiFragile Music and released his first album under the label, *Majesto Sessions*, alongside his debut novel, *The Lights Along Majesto*.
Kate Addo (born July 23, 1972) is a Ghanaian journalist, broadcaster, and public relations practitioner, currently serving as the Director of Public Affairs for the Parliament of Ghana.

Don Sweeney (born August 17, 1966) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey player and current executive. A defenseman, he played 16 seasons in the NHL, primarily with the Boston Bruins, appearing in 1,115 games and recording 273 points. Sweeney also played briefly for the Dallas Stars before retiring in 2004. He was named general manager of the Bruins in 2015, overseeing key trades and guiding the team to the 2019 Stanley Cup Finals. Sweeney was awarded the NHL General Manager of the Year Award in 2019. He is married to former figure skater Christine Hough.

The Neradje Mosque (built 1890) is a former mosque in Rethymno, Crete, Greece. It was built by Georgios Daskalakis during Ottoman rule. It previously was a Catholic monastery. Today it is a music school.

Norma Aleandro (born May 2, 1936) is an acclaimed Argentine actress, screenwriter, and playwright. Born in Buenos Aires to a family of actors, she began her career in 1952 and became one of Argentina's most celebrated performers. Exiled during the military dictatorship of the late 1970s due to her progressive views, she lived in Uruguay and Spain before returning to Argentina in 1983.

Aleandro gained international recognition for her role in the Academy Award-winning film *The Official Story* (1985), earning the Cannes Award for Best Actress. She also appeared in notable Argentine films such as *Son of the Bride*, *Sol de Otoño*, and *El Faro*, as well as Hollywood productions like *Gaby: A True Story* (1987), for which she received an Academy Award nomination.

A prominent stage actress, Aleandro won the María Guerrero Award in 1996 and was named an Illustrious Citizen of Buenos Aires the same year.

Waingaro is a rural hamlet in the Waikato District of New Zealand, known for its hot springs and marae. With a population of 189 (2018), it features Waingaro School and historic Waingaro Landing, once a key transport route.

Attabad Lake, also known as Gojal Lake, is a lake in the Hunza Valley of Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan, formed on January 4, 2010, by a massive landslide in Attabad village. The disaster blocked the Hunza River, creating a 21-kilometer-long lake that displaced thousands, submerged villages, and disrupted the Karakoram Highway. The lake has since become a significant tourist attraction.

George Ware (1924–2010) was an American botanist and dendrologist known for his work at the Morton Arboretum, where he researched hybrid trees, particularly disease-resistant elms, and led botanical expeditions to China and the Soviet Union.

Kimbolton is a civil parish and town in Cambridgeshire, England, with a population of 1,477 as of the 2011 census. Historically significant, it was the estate of King Harold and is mentioned in the Domesday Book. The town features Kimbolton Castle, once home to Catherine of Aragon, and the Grade I listed St Andrew's Church. Kimbolton hosts the annual Statute Fair, a tradition dating back to the 13th century, and is known for local amenities including shops, pubs, and a medical center.

*Ebirah, Horror of the Deep* (released in the United States as *Godzilla versus the Sea Monster*) is a 1966 Japanese kaiju film directed by Jun Fukuda and produced by Toho Co., Ltd. The film stars Akira Takarada, Kumi Mizuno, and Chotaro Togin, with special effects directed by Sadamasa Arikawa under the supervision of Eiji Tsuburaya. It is the seventh installment in the *Godzilla* franchise and follows a group of castaways who discover a terrorist organization, the Red Bamboo, enslaving natives on Letchi Island while using a giant lobster-like kaiju, Ebirah, to guard their operations. The group awakens Godzilla to battle Ebirah and ultimately thwart the Red Bamboo's plans.

Originally conceived as a King Kong film, the project was reworked to feature Godzilla after creative differences with Rankin/Bass. Released in Japan on December 17, 1966, the film was later distributed in the U.S. and gained cult status, including a feature on *Mystery Science Theater 3000*.

Mansfield is a borough in Tioga County, Pennsylvania, United States, settled in 1804 and incorporated in 1857. It is home to Mansfield University of Pennsylvania and is notable for hosting the first night football game under electric lights in 1892.

HIV/AIDS in Pakistan  

HIV/AIDS in Pakistan has evolved from a low-prevalence epidemic to a concentrated one, primarily affecting people who inject drugs (PWID) and transgender sex workers. The national HIV prevalence among PWID is 27.2%, while transgender sex workers have a prevalence of 5.2%. Female sex workers and the general population remain at lower risk, though unsafe medical injections and limited surveillance pose ongoing threats.  

The epidemic emerged in the early 2000s, with the first major outbreak among injection drug users in 2003. High-risk behaviors, cultural stigma, and insufficient healthcare monitoring complicate accurate case tracking. The government's response includes prevention programs, blood screening, and antiretroviral treatment, though coverage remains low. Future challenges include improving intervention effectiveness, reducing stigma, and integrating HIV services into broader healthcare systems.

Chas Henry (born January 6, 1989) is a former American football punter who played in the NFL for the Philadelphia Eagles (2011–2012). A consensus All-American and Ray Guy Award winner at the University of Florida, he was part of the Gators' 2008 BCS national championship team. Henry also briefly played for the Tampa Bay Buccaneers in 2013.

"Fear" is the third studio album by American alternative rock band Toad the Wet Sprocket, released on August 27, 1991. Recorded at Granny's House in Reno, Nevada, the album features a total length of 46 minutes and 37 seconds and was produced by Gavin MacKillop. "Fear" includes notable singles such as "All I Want," "Walk on the Ocean," and "Hold Her Down." The album was financed by Columbia Records, which also supported the mixing of fourteen tracks, ultimately releasing twelve. Two additional songs from the recording sessions, "Good Intentions" and "All in All," later appeared on the 1995 rarities collection "In Light Syrup."

Emmanuel III Delly (27 September 1927 – 8 April 2014) was an Iraqi cardinal of the Chaldean Catholic Church who served as Patriarch of Babylon of the Chaldeans from 2003 to 2012. Born in Tel Keppe, Iraq, he was ordained a priest in 1952 and became a bishop in 1963. As patriarch, he led the largest Christian community in Iraq and worked to foster interfaith dialogue, particularly with Muslim leaders. Elevated to the College of Cardinals in 2007 by Pope Benedict XVI, Delly contributed to the global Chaldean Church through the construction of churches and religious institutions. He retired in 2012 and died in San Diego, California, in 2014.

Give Notice (foaled May 18, 1997) was a British-bred gelding racehorse trained by John Dunlop. He was sired by Warning and had a successful racing career, achieving a record of 27 starts with 7 wins, 3 seconds, and 4 thirds, earning £185,219. Notable victories include the Stubbs Stakes and the Sagaro Stakes in 2002, as well as the prestigious Prix du Cadran later that year. Give Notice was known for his stamina and competitive spirit, particularly in long-distance races.

Zach Gage (born 1985) is an American independent video game developer and artist based in New York. Known for his innovative game designs, Gage's work spans mobile apps, interactive installations, and digital adaptations of traditional games.

As a child, Gage created games using drawing software and Apple's HyperCard. He later earned an art degree from Skidmore College and an MFA from Parsons School of Design. Gage's notable games include SpellTower, a commercially successful word game, and #Fortune, an app that generates fortunes based on Twitter messages. He also worked on the iOS version of Ridiculous Fishing and created digital versions of card games like Sage Solitaire and Really Bad Chess.

Henri Murger (1822–1861) was a French novelist and poet, best known for *Scènes de la vie de bohème* (1851), which depicted the lives of impoverished artists in Paris and later inspired Puccini’s opera *La bohème*. Born to a working-class family, Murger worked various menial jobs before gaining literary recognition with the support of writer Étienne de Jouy. Though he initially wrote poetry and journalism, his greatest success came from fiction, particularly his vivid portrayals of bohemian life. Despite his literary achievements, Murger struggled with financial instability and poor health. He received the Légion d'honneur in 1859 but died in poverty two years later at age 38. His funeral, funded by the French government, was attended by prominent cultural figures. Early in his career, he adopted the spelling "Henry Murger" to appear more distinctive, though he later dropped the German-influenced "Mürger" variant.

Nodes of Ranvier is an American metalcore and Christian metal band from Sioux Falls, South Dakota, formed in 2000. The band released three albums under Facedown Records before signing with Victory Records in 2007. Despite their initial success, they disbanded later that year, with guitarist Jon Parker as the only remaining original member. In 2017, the band reunited to perform at Facedown Fest, celebrating the label's 20th anniversary. Over its history, the band has featured 18 different members.

Dhrangadhra is a town in Gujarat, India, known as the "Pink City." Established as the capital of the Jhalawad Princely State in 1735, it has a rich history dating back to the Lower Paleolithic period. With a population of 75,133 as of 2011, Dhrangadhra features diverse industries, including the Dhrangadhra Chemical Works, and is recognized for its cultural heritage, including numerous temples and historical sites.
Joseph Buck is an American soccer midfielder currently playing for the Georgetown Hoyas, having previously played for New England Revolution II.

Sportpalast speech  

The Sportpalast speech (or "Total War" speech) was a public address delivered by Nazi propaganda minister Joseph Goebbels at the Berlin Sportpalast on 18 February 1943, amid Germany's mounting losses in World War II. Following major Axis defeats at Stalingrad and in North Africa, Goebbels sought to rally public support for a total war effort. The speech, marked by its aggressive rhetoric, framed the conflict as a struggle against "International Jewry" and Bolshevism, while demanding greater sacrifices from the German people. Goebbels justified recent austerity measures and called for complete mobilization, famously asking the audience a series of leading questions to affirm their commitment to victory. The event was carefully staged, with a handpicked crowd and prominent Nazi banners, including the slogan *"Totaler Krieg – kürzester Krieg"* ("Total War – Shortest War"). Though the speech initially failed to secure Goebbels full control over the war economy, it became a defining moment in Nazi propaganda. Historians note its significance in escalating Germany's war mobilization and its explicit references to the regime's genocidal policies.
Nicholas N. Cox (1837-1912) was a U.S. Representative from Tennessee, serving in Congress for five terms from 1891 to 1901. He was also a Confederate officer during the Civil War.

The November 1992 Venezuelan coup attempt was the second coup attempt that year against President Carlos Andrés Pérez. Led by air force and navy officers, the rebels took over a state-run television station and seized air bases. The coup ultimately failed, resulting in 172 deaths, and many participants fled to Peru.

Freienfeld (Italian: Campo di Trens) is a comune (municipality) in South Tyrol in northern Italy, located in the Eisacktal. As of 30 November 2010, it had a population of 2,668 and an area of 95.3 square kilometres (36.8 sq mi).
Casey's theorem states a relationship involving four non-intersecting circles tangent to a larger circle, providing a formula for the length of their exterior common bitangent.

HMS *Rover* was a 16-gun sloop-of-war of the Royal Navy, launched in 1796 and wrecked in the Gulf of St. Lawrence in 1798. Designed by Goodrich & Co. and built in Bermuda, she was initially planned as a brig but was lengthened and rigged as a full ship during construction. Commissioned in 1798 under Commander George Irwin, *Rover* captured the French privateer *Jean Bart* shortly before her loss. She was wrecked off Cape Breton Island while convoying a transport, with all crew surviving.
Sangita Patel (born January 2, 1979) is a Canadian television personality. She is a co-host on Entertainment Tonight Canada.

*Kamaal the Abstract* is the second studio album by American rapper and producer Q-Tip, released on September 15, 2009, through Battery Records. Originally recorded in 2001, the album was shelved by Arista Records due to concerns over its commercial viability but gained a cult following after promotional copies leaked online. The album marks a departure from Q-Tip's mainstream-oriented debut, *Amplified* (1999), showcasing an eclectic blend of neo-soul, jazz fusion, funk, and hip hop, with improvisational arrangements and introspective lyrics. Its instrumentation features electric piano, flute, deep organs, and guitar grooves, drawing comparisons to works by Prince, D'Angelo, and the Soulquarians collective.

Upon its eventual release, *Kamaal the Abstract* received generally positive reviews for its experimental sound, though some critics noted its uneven execution. It debuted at number 77 on the Billboard 200 and includes the single "Barely in Love." The album is considered a bold, genre-defying effort in Q-Tip's discography.

Terry Mutchler is an American journalist and attorney known for her pioneering work in media and government transparency. She began her career at The Morning Call and later became the first female Statehouse Correspondent for the Associated Press in 1993. Mutchler served as the first director of the Pennsylvania Office of Open Records, where she significantly improved public access to information. She has represented various clients in legal matters, including high-profile cases involving allegations of domestic abuse. Mutchler is also an author, with her memoir detailing a secret relationship with Illinois Senator Penny Severns.

Rüdiger Sagel (born 1957) is a German politician and activist. Initially a member of the Greens, he later joined the Left Party and served in the Landtag of North Rhine-Westphalia (1998–2007, 2010–2012). A longtime anti-nuclear and environmental campaigner, he co-founded the Hambach-Gruppe against surface mining and participated in protests like the Free Republic of Wendland. He also worked on social and cultural projects in Nicaragua and Latin America.

The Wesley Bolin Memorial Plaza is a public space in Phoenix, Arizona, established on March 9, 1978, and named after Governor Wesley Bolin. Located near the state capitol, it features 30 monuments and memorials, including tributes to the USS *Arizona*, various wars, and the United States Bill of Rights. The plaza has served as a site for public gatherings, protests, and demonstrations, including the 2006 immigration reform rally. Some memorials, such as the Ten Commandments monument, the Arizona 9/11 Memorial, and the Arizona Confederate Troops Memorial, have sparked controversy over the years.

Neodiprion abietis, commonly known as the balsam fir sawfly, is an insect species in the family Diprionidae. It primarily feeds on the foliage of balsam fir and other conifers, with larvae causing significant defoliation during outbreaks. The species exhibits a unique reproductive strategy, allowing unmated females to produce male offspring through parthenogenesis. N. abietis outbreaks can last several years, impacting tree growth and health, particularly in eastern Canada.

*System of a Down* is the debut studio album by American metal band System of a Down, released on June 30, 1998, through American Recordings and Columbia Records. Produced by Rick Rubin and the band, the album blends nu metal and alternative metal with themes of war, genocide, religion, and brainwashing. It received critical acclaim and is featured in *1001 Albums You Must Hear Before You Die*.

Yann Weymouth is an American architect known for his role as chief of design for I.M. Pei on the National Gallery of Art East Wing in Washington, D.C., and the Grand Louvre Project in Paris, including the iconic Louvre Pyramid. A graduate of Harvard University and MIT, he has held prominent positions at firms such as Arup, SOM, Arquitectonica, and HOK Architects, where he served as senior vice president and design director. Weymouth designed notable projects including the Salvador Dalí Museum in Florida and expansions of the John and Mable Ringling Museum of Art. He has been honored as a “Chevalier” and later “Officier” of the Ordre National du Mérite by France. Weymouth is the brother of musician Tina Weymouth and the great-grandson of Breton writer Anatole Le Braz. He has been married twice and has three children, including Katharine Weymouth.

Hope Hibbard (December 18, 1893 – May 12, 1988) was an American zoologist and professor. She earned degrees from the University of Missouri and Bryn Mawr College, culminating in a Ph.D. in zoology in 1921. After a Sarah Berliner Fellowship and further study at the Sorbonne, she joined Oberlin College in 1928, where she remained for 34 years, becoming a full professor and department chair.

Hibbard's research spanned cytology, histology, and marine biology, with a focus on marine invertebrates and the Golgi apparatus. She was also a trustee at the Woods Hole Marine Biological Laboratory. Beyond her scientific pursuits, Hibbard was an advocate for women in science, urging them to integrate marriage and professional endeavors. Oberlin College established the Hope Hibbard Memorial Award in her honor.
Cipher runes are encrypted runic inscriptions, primarily preserved in Icelandic manuscripts, that use structured patterns to conceal messages.

Joseph Tate is an American attorney who graduated from Villanova University in 1963 and its School of Law in 1966. He served as Lewis Libby's attorney during the CIA leak grand jury investigation, publicly denying Libby's involvement as journalist Judith Miller's confidential source. Tate maintained that Libby had provided a written waiver, and Miller was eventually released from jail after a phone call facilitated by their attorneys.

The Faculty of Informatics and Statistics at the University of Economics in Prague, established in 1991, is a public institution located in Prague, Czech Republic. Led by Dean prof. Ing. Jakub Fischer, Ph.D., the faculty has 2,490 students and offers Bachelor, Master, and doctoral programs in fields such as Informatics, Econometrics, and Statistics. It collaborates with institutions like the Czech Technical University, the Academy of Sciences, the Czech Statistical Office, and international universities.

Shigeichi Nagano (1925 – January 30, 2019) was a Japanese photographer known for his work capturing the lives of Tokyo's salarymen during Japan's economic growth. He also contributed to film and television, notably as a cinematographer for the film Tokyo Olympiad.

*Baankey Ki Crazy Baraat* is a 2015 Indian Hindi-language comedy film directed by Aijaz Khan and written by Anita Mani. Starring Rajpal Yadav, Sanjay Mishra, Vijay Raaz, and Tia Bajpai, the film follows a bachelor whose family arranges a proxy groom for his wedding due to astrological issues. Despite its quirky premise and seasoned cast, the film received mixed to negative reviews, with critics praising performances but criticizing the convoluted plot and lack of humor.

*I, Olga Hepnarová* is a 2016 Czech biographical drama film directed by Tomáš Weinreb and Petr Kazda. Starring Michalina Olszańska, the film explores the life and crimes of Olga Hepnarová, a woman who committed a mass murder in 1973. Praised for its stark cinematography and haunting narrative, it received mixed to positive reviews, with a 78% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes.

Dr. Horace Edward Dobbs (born January 14, 1933) is a British scientist, researcher, and author known for his work in dolphin behavior and conservation. He has published several books, including *Follow a Wild Dolphin*, and is a prominent advocate against dolphin captivity. Dobbs has also created a series of fictional children's books about an orphaned dolphin named Dilo.

SPINDLE is a proposed autonomous cryobot and submersible being developed by Stone Aerospace, intended to melt through thick ice sheets to explore subglacial lakes and oceans. The project integrates technology from previous projects, including the VALKYRIE cryobot and the ARTEMIS submersible. Unlike VALKYRIE, SPINDLE will melt ice by beaming a laser directly into it.

SPINDLE is designed to penetrate 1.5 to 4 km (0.93 to 2.49 mi) through ice, while the ARTEMIS submersible can explore up to one kilometer (0.62 mi) radius from the cryobot. It will feature radar and an intelligent algorithm for autonomous navigation and scientific sampling. SPINDLE is designed to be bi-directional and vertically controllable, allowing it to return to the surface. The project is viewed as a precursor to future missions to icy moons like Europa and Enceladus, to explore their subsurface oceans and search for potential life. For missions to icy moons, the design includes radio receiver buoys to be deposited into the ice during descent.
Republic Township is a civil township in Marquette County, Michigan, covering an area of 119.54 square miles. As of the 2010 census, it had a population of 1,060 residents.
The rusty-margined guan (Penelope superciliaris) is a species of bird in the family Cracidae. It is found in South America in a variety of landscapes. The IUCN has assessed it as near threatened.

Marilyn Suiter is an American geoscientist recognized for her contributions to geosciences education and diversity. She earned her degrees from Franklin & Marshall College and Wesleyan University, and has held significant roles at the American Geosciences Institute and the National Science Foundation, promoting minority participation in the field.
Lady Juliana was a British barque launched in 1777, known for transporting female convicts to Australia in 1789 and later trading until 1829.

The 1952 Mysore State Legislative Assembly election was held on March 26, 1952, to elect members to the 99-seat assembly. The Indian National Congress (INC) won the election, with K. Hanumanthaiah becoming the Chief Minister, succeeding K. Chengalaraya Reddy.

Middle Scots is a historical form of the Scots language that developed in the Scottish Lowlands and parts of the Northern Isles, evolving into Modern Scots by the mid-18th century. The term "Scottis" was adopted in the early 16th century to refer to the national language of the Stewart kingdom, distinguishing it from Gaelic, which became known as "Erse." Middle Scots experienced significant changes due to political and social influences, particularly following the Union of Parliaments in 1707, when Modern English began to dominate literary contexts. The language is characterized by its variable orthography and distinct phonological developments, including vowel length rules and mergers that differentiate it from contemporary southern English.

Theodorick Bland (December 2, 1708 – June 1, 1784) was an American planter, politician, and member of the Virginia Senate from 1776 to 1779, representing Isle of Wight, Surry, and Prince George Counties. A member of the prominent Bland and Randolph families, he managed several plantations, including Kippax and Cawsons, and later moved to Amelia County. Bland played a role in supporting the American Revolution, including raising funds to replace gunpowder seized by British forces. He was also a militia colonel and descended from Theodorick Bland of Westover, a notable early Virginian.

**Coryphodon** is an extinct genus of large, slow-moving mammals that lived during the Late Paleocene to Middle Eocene, approximately 57 to 46 million years ago. Belonging to the order Pantodonta, it was one of the largest mammals of its time, with a body length of 2.5 meters (8.2 feet) and a weight ranging from 340 to 700 kilograms (750 to 1,500 pounds). Despite its size, Coryphodon had a small brain relative to its body. It likely had a semi-aquatic lifestyle, inhabiting swamps and marshes, and its diet varied seasonally, including plants, fungi, and leaf litter. Fossils have been found as far north as Ellesmere Island, revealing adaptations to Arctic conditions.

Hazel Hawke AO (née Masterson; 20 July 1929 – 23 May 2013) was an Australian activist, musician, and writer. She was the wife of Prime Minister Bob Hawke (1983–1991) and a prominent advocate for women's rights, Alzheimer's awareness, and the arts. Diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease in 2001, she publicly shared her experience to raise awareness. She was honored as an Officer of the Order of Australia for her community contributions. Hawke died in 2013 at age 83.

*Wizzard Brew* is the debut studio album by the British rock band Wizzard, released in 1973. Produced by Roy Wood, the album features a blend of art rock and rock and roll, characterized by experimental techniques such as ring modulation. The album received mixed critical and popular reactions, with some distinguishing its experimental nature from the accessibility of Wizzard's singles. A remastered edition with bonus tracks was released in 2006.
Workpoint TV is a Thai television network owned by Workpoint Entertainment, launched in 2011, offering various entertainment and news programming.

Caesium bisulfate (CsHSO4) is a superionic conductor that exhibits significant proton conductivity above 141 °C, transitioning through three crystalline phases. Its unique properties make it suitable for potential applications in electrochemical devices.
Elías Contreras (born March 7, 1997) is an Argentine professional footballer who plays as a right midfielder for Brown Adrogué, on loan from Independiente.

Anthony Di Pietro (born February 15, 1969) is an Australian businessman and the chairman of Melbourne Victory FC since 2011. He is also the Group CEO of Premier Fresh Australia, which operates across major Australian markets. Di Pietro has played a significant role in Melbourne Victory's success, overseeing multiple championships and high-profile events, including a record crowd match against Liverpool F.C. in 2013.

In computational geometry, an optimal bitonic tour is a bitonic tour of minimum total length. Dynamic programming can construct the optimal bitonic tour in polynomial time. The optimal bitonic tour has no self-crossings and minimizes horizontal motion.

George Washington's reception at Trenton was a celebration on April 21, 1789, when he passed through Trenton, New Jersey, on his way to New York City for his presidential inauguration. He was greeted at the Assunpink Creek bridge by the "Ladies of Trenton," who sang and spread flowers before him. A triumphal arch was erected, and a dinner was held at the City Tavern. The event has been depicted in numerous artworks and was reenacted in 1989.

Theodorick Bland (December 2, 1708 – June 1, 1784) was a Virginia planter and politician who served in the Virginia Senate from 1776 to 1779. Born into a prominent family, he operated Kippax and Cawsons plantations and played a role in the American Revolutionary War.

Henry Stuart Russell (1818 – 5 March 1889) was an English-born Australian explorer, pastoralist, and politician. He is known for his exploration of Queensland, including the discovery of the Mary and Boyne Rivers, and for his book *The Genesis of Queensland* (1888), which documented the region's early settlement and pastoral industry. Russell also served in the New South Wales Legislative Council and held various pastoral interests before returning to England later in life.

*The Harold Peary Show* was an American radio situation comedy that aired on CBS from 1950 to 1951. Starring Harold Peary as "Honest Harold" Hemp, the show struggled to replicate the success of *The Great Gildersleeve*, with which it shared notable similarities. Despite its humor and ensemble cast, the program failed to gain a significant audience and ended after one season.

John Leslie Sissons (born 30 September 1945) is an English former footballer who played as a forward for West Ham United, Sheffield Wednesday, Norwich City, and Chelsea. He was the youngest player to score in an FA Cup Final at Wembley in 1964.

The 1945 Katsuyama killing incident occurred in Okinawa, Japan, during the U.S. occupation after the Battle of Okinawa. Three African-American Marines, accused of repeatedly raping village women, were ambushed and killed by villagers with the help of Japanese soldiers. Their bodies were hidden in a cave and remained undiscovered until 1997. The case was not criminally investigated, and the incident has been cited by critics of the U.S. military presence in Okinawa as an example of postwar tensions.
Domingos Duarte (born 10 March 1995) is a Portuguese professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Getafe CF and the Portugal national team.
Sophie Cook (born 4 January 1967) is a British transgender activist, journalist, and former RAF technician.

The 2012 West Oxfordshire District Council election was held on 3 May 2012 to elect members of West Oxfordshire District Council in England. The Conservative Party retained control of the council despite losing three seats to Labour, ending with 41 councillors. Labour and the Liberal Democrats each held four seats, while voter turnout averaged 34.77%.

Abu Thumama al-Sa'idi (died October 10, 680 AD) was a prominent Shi'a companion of Ali ibn Abi Talib and his grandsons, Hassan and Hussain. Noted for his exceptional horsemanship, he played a significant role in the events leading up to the Battle of Karbala, where he was killed while fighting valiantly for Hussain ibn Ali on the day of Ashura.

Getafe Air Base (Base Aérea de Getafe) is a military airfield located in Getafe, Spain, operated by the Spanish Air and Space Force. Established in 1911, the base is situated at an elevation of 2,034 feet (620 meters) and features a single runway designated 05/23. In addition to its military functions, Getafe Air Base is adjacent to facilities for the assembly and maintenance of aircraft, including the Spanish Eurofighter Typhoon. The base also hosts the "Ala 35" transport wing, which operates various aircraft for military transport and VIP missions. Future plans include the relocation of the Infante de Orleans Foundation to the base, as well as proposals for a civil aerodrome, which have faced opposition from the local government due to concerns about pollution.
Animal Control Products is a New Zealand state-owned enterprise, established in 1991, that manufactures and distributes pest control products for various government and commercial clients.

Budha Subba Temple is a Hindu temple located in Dharan, Nepal, dedicated to a legendary figure associated with local folklore. According to myth, it marks the meditation site of a hunter named Budha Subba, who abandoned hunting after a failed shot with his slingshot. The temple is also linked to the last Limbu King of Limbuwan, Budhhi Karna Khebang, who was killed in 1773 and later revered as a benevolent spirit. Another legend connects it to Lord Shiva and Parvati, while some associate it with the Mahabharata's Eklavya. The site is known for its bamboo trees, which lack tips and are traditionally inscribed with lovers' names—though this practice has been restricted to protect the trees. Today, devotees tie sacred threads to the bamboo instead. The temple remains a popular pilgrimage and picnic spot.

Argentina competed at the inaugural 1960 Summer Paralympics in Rome, Italy, with a delegation of five athletes, all participating in swimming events. The team achieved significant success, winning a total of six medals: two gold, three silver, and one bronze, ranking 10th in the medal table. Notable performances included Sznitowski, who won a gold and a silver medal, and Perazzo, who claimed a gold medal in the women's 50 metres crawl incomplete class 3. All athletes competed in events for individuals with spinal cord injuries requiring wheelchairs.
